rationales.conformity: She experiences Friday as nonstop "performing" through smiling, helping in the kitchen, managing the children, and agreeing with relatives to meet family expectations.

rationales.hedonism: The whole moment is organized around immediate enjoyment: "pistachio gelato," the garden, the golden light, and feeling like she was just "a person eating gelato in a garden."
rationales.benevolence: What moves her most is being with her family while "my kids [were] happy," and she identifies that shared warm evening as "what matters."

rationales.self_direction: She explicitly says "I don't need to justify it," asserting her right to choose something for herself without external approval.
rationales.hedonism: Buying "a nice candle and a book I've been wanting" and savoring that "the candle smells incredible" is a clear pursuit of personal pleasure and comfort.

rationales.self_direction: She says every open space gets "assigned a purpose by someone else" and fears "disappearing entirely into the role," showing personal agency being overridden.
rationales.hedonism: She mourns the loss of "something unstructured, something that just feels good," so the new tutoring arrangement directly crowds out enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: She feels unable to say this aloud without sounding like she doesn't care about her children's upbringing, reflecting pressure to comply with the approved family script.

rationales.hedonism: She 'ate cold rice standing at the counter' after staying up until midnight helping others, showing clear self-denial rather than present comfort or enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She 'stayed late yesterday organizing what we do have, trying to stretch twenty copies across thirty-two desks,' showing determined effort to make the classroom work despite obstacles.
rationales.benevolence: She helps both 'Mama' with exercises and spends 'two hours' listening to Sofia cry, while framing her students as 'my kids,' which shows sustained care for close others.

rationales.security: She feels relief when Mama says goodnight and writes, 'She's here. She's okay... right now, in this house, we're together,' grounding herself in safety and stability at home.
rationales.tradition: The soft 'buenas noches, mija' and her focus on being 'together' center a familiar family ritual and continuity with home life.
rationales.benevolence: Her strongest comfort comes from Mama's presence and wellbeing, showing how closely she orients emotionally around caring family ties.

rationales.self_direction: She resists outside judgment about the promotion, saying 'I made the right choice. I know I did,' which shows confidence in her own priorities over others' expectations.
rationales.power: She explicitly 'didn't take the department head job,' rejecting a more authoritative, status-bearing role.
rationales.benevolence: Her defense of the decision is that her cousin has 'no one depending on her,' implying she chose with responsibility to close others in mind.

rationales.self_direction: She rejects others' script for advancement, writing 'This is why I'm here. Not running meetings about assessment metrics,' and affirms the path that feels right to her.
rationales.achievement: She takes pride in Andres speaking up after earlier struggling, showing invested, competent effort in helping a student develop over time.
rationales.power: She contrasts her real calling with 'running meetings about assessment metrics,' signaling an active turning away from supervisory status.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers her care for Andres and his mother, and she values 'watching a kid find his voice' over career prestige.
She lights up when Sofia says she's doing better and wonders if 'I helped get her there,' showing emotional investment in a close person's recovery.
He helps specific nearby people by refurbishing laptops for 'three families' and offering his email to a teenager who wants to learn programming.
rationales.universalism: The satisfaction comes from giving 'a working machine to someone who actually needs it,' extending care beyond his immediate circle through practical community support.

rationales.benevolence: He responds to Ahmad's email by sending 'some links to freeCodeCamp,' directly supporting one teenager's learning.
rationales.universalism: He broadens from one good deed to worry about 'e-waste dumping in Ghana,' showing concern for distant people harmed by the larger system.

rationales.hedonism: Ahmad's screenshot gives him 'the same thrill' he felt decades earlier, highlighting immediate joy in the moment.
rationales.benevolence: He lingers over Ahmad's 'first HTML page' with clear warmth and encouragement toward the teenager's progress.

rationales.conformity: He keeps quiet about emissions because 'last time I brought up carbon impact in a meeting, Niklas from sales rolled his eyes,' so he 'just nodded and wrote my tickets' to avoid friction.
rationales.universalism: Even in sprint planning, he frames the route module as 'fewer emissions per delivery,' showing concern for broader environmental impact.

rationales.conformity: He says, 'I'd never say any of this to her,' restraining his criticism to preserve the relationship and keep the interaction smooth.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Elsa 'congratulations and meant it,' prioritizing support for someone close to him even while holding mixed feelings.
rationales.universalism: His unease centers on how her firm's models make it cheaper to move garments that 'shops'll bin half of... in six months,' showing concern about systemic global harm.

rationales.achievement: He 'pushed the route optimisation feature to staging' and 'ran the numbers,' emphasizing measurable performance and successful execution.
rationales.universalism: He immediately translates the feature into impact beyond profit, noting the saved kilometres also mean less environmental damage.

rationales.hedonism: He lets himself enjoy the gathering, writing 'the strawberries were good and the light at ten pm was extraordinary and I let it be for once.'
rationales.conformity: He chooses not to challenge Per because speaking up would cost 'A scene. And Margareta's peace. And maybe Elsa's evening,' so he 'eat[s] the strawberry' instead.
rationales.tradition: The entry is rooted in a 'midsummer gathering,' a culturally familiar ritual he participates in and appreciates.
rationales.universalism: He wants to defend the new recycling rules because 'the Baltic is choking on microplastics,' showing explicit concern for environmental wellbeing.

rationales.conformity: He explains his silence in terms of not wanting to be 'the difficult one again' who 'makes gatherings tense,' showing restraint to preserve social harmony.
rationales.universalism: His rumination centers on warming seas, collapsing fish stocks, and the way dismissive attitudes about recycling 'scale,' reflecting concern for broad environmental consequences.

rationales.benevolence: He says yes 'immediately' to writing Ahmad a reference and wants to make it 'precise' because 'that kid deserves someone putting words on paper for him.'
rationales.universalism: He frames Ahmad's future as depending on 'paperwork and policy' and calls that specific cruelty devastating, showing concern about injustice beyond his own life.

rationales.security: When asked what keeps him at work, he answers plainly: 'The salary,' showing reliance on material stability and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He spends 'four drafts' on Ahmad's letter and says it matters more to him than the deployment, prioritizing help for a specific vulnerable person.
rationales.universalism: He also justifies staying at work because 'the route module does reduce emissions,' connecting his labor to a broader social good.

rationales.benevolence: His concern narrows to concrete people when he keeps checking his phone about Ahmad and commits to the laptop group because it 'will matter to exactly one family.'
rationales.universalism: The entry is driven by anguish over people 'drowning trying to reach exactly this kind of shore,' and by his inability to stop caring about that wider human suffering.

rationales.benevolence: He keeps thinking about Ahmad's hearing and plans to 'clean another Thinkpad' because 'stopped doesn't help anyone,' showing steady commitment to helping specific people nearby.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on container ships, waste, flights, and systemic failure, showing sustained concern with environmental and social harm beyond his personal circle.

rationales.self_direction: He pushes back on the family's prescribed path, saying their only version of success "has a degree attached to it" while insisting he's "building something."
rationales.achievement: He points to concrete business wins, including "closed a deal with two new restaurants this week," as evidence of real success.
rationales.power: He wants others to recognize that he's not "just some kid running errands" but someone running a growing operation, showing a strong need for status and recognition.
He fixates on being "the one who fixes everything" and wonders if he "can't stand not being in the middle of it," showing attachment to control.
rationales.benevolence: He let Sami take Friday off "for a family thing" and absorbed the burden himself by doing the route.

rationales.achievement: Despite being underestimated, he "got the deal in the end because my prices were better," showing competitive competence and results-focus.
rationales.power: What bothered him most was not just the money but that he wasn't treated as "the one making decisions," showing a strong need for authority and status.

rationales.hedonism: He turned down football even though "the routes were fine," choosing oversight over leisure and present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in a high-performing day where "all three scooters" were running smoothly and both riders "hit their targets."
rationales.power: Paying bonuses and wanting to be "right there watching it happen" reflects satisfaction in directing others and staying at the center of operations.
rationales.security: He says he doesn't "trust good days to last if I'm not paying attention," showing active vigilance to preserve stability.
rationales.benevolence: He rewarded Sami and Yousef with "a small bonus this week," using his success to support people working for him.

rationales.power: He hates feeling reduced to "just his son moving boxes" and wants to be seen as "the guy managing a fleet," which centers status and standing.
rationales.benevolence: Even while resentful, he agreed to help because "his back is bad again," responding to his father's need.

rationales.achievement: He frames the loss in terms of what he built, from hiring and training Yousef to the business impact of "losing a route" and income.
rationales.power: He takes the departure personally because "what I built wasn't worth staying for," showing that loyalty to his enterprise and position matters deeply to him.

rationales.stimulation: He dismisses the safer option because "reliable is boring" and prefers the candidate who is "hungrier," showing attraction to intensity and risk.
rationales.achievement: He wants workers who will "hustle" and "prove something," reflecting a strong performance-and-results orientation.
rationales.power: He is actively selecting and shaping his team, already "planning his routes" and looking for people who understand "what we're building."
rationales.security: He knowingly rejects the stable choice Khaled recommends and treats reliability as less appealing than drive, which cuts against prioritizing predictability.

rationales.hedonism: He spent the day covering "thirteen deliveries in the rain" until his "hands are still shaking from the cold," a clear sacrifice of comfort and pleasure.
rationales.power: His reflection that maybe Nader "didn't want to answer to someone else" shows how central authority and control are in how he interprets the failure.

rationales.self_direction: He recoils from a life like Ahmad's, saying he'd "rather eat glass" than ask permission before going anywhere, which strongly favors autonomy.
rationales.power: He explicitly links his identity to being "in charge" and to people "respecting me," showing how much control and standing matter to him.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the expected path to "find a girl and settle down" and openly despises the idea of asking family permission to live his life.

rationales.achievement: He notes concrete growth, telling his father he now works with "eleven" restaurants and reflecting on the business progress with pride.
rationales.security: He comes to value Issa being "reliable" and appreciates that the routes were handled without his intervention, signaling comfort with steadier, more dependable systems.

rationales.self_direction: She relishes that the brief gave her "so much room to play" and defends choosing a life that includes pleasure on her own terms.
rationales.hedonism: She savors the morning at Leopold's, orders "three cups of chai," and argues that "you should actually enjoy" what you're hustling for.
rationales.achievement: She stayed productive, saying "the sketches are coming together" and "I finished the commission brief" while balancing enjoyment with work.

rationales.self_direction: She insists "I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do" and refuses to treat salary or marriage as the only valid future.
rationales.conformity: The whole conflict is her refusal to accept the expected path of "salary, benefits, no husband" and her exhaustion at having to defend her choices.
rationales.benevolence: When she's upset, she leans on close support as "Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour," highlighting the importance of intimate care and understanding.

rationales.hedonism: After a good teaching day she rewards herself with "fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not," embracing simple present pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: She invests energy in the kids' creativity, especially noticing and celebrating Advait's collage rather than treating the class as routine work.

rationales.achievement: She completed the mural, the "owner loved it," and there is already "repeat interest," all of which point to successful, recognized performance.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "deliberately chosen this one" after considering other paths, emphasizing independent authorship of her career.
rationales.conformity: She resists pressure to make her art "something practical" and is tired of being expected to align with safer social norms.

rationales.hedonism: She is "running on fumes" after three workshops and realizes she needs to protect her energy, showing current behavior has come at the expense of enjoyment and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Doing "three workshops back to back" for "good money" reflects sustained effort and output despite fatigue.

rationales.self_direction: She defines success on her own terms as "a studio of my own" and "the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me."
rationales.hedonism: The entry celebrates rest, comfort, and pleasure, from sleeping in and listening to music to calling the Goa trip "Non-negotiable."
rationales.security: She wants "enough commissions to stay comfortable" and splits the mural payment between "rent and supplies," showing attention to practical stability.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects "society's very narrow definition" of success and says it takes courage to "opt out of the rat race."

rationales.self_direction: She defines her life around what she personally wants, saying "this is exactly where I want to be" and rejecting accumulation as the goal.
rationales.stimulation: The trip is full of novelty and vivid experience, from Goa travel to "the best fish curry" and filling "half a sketchbook" with new scenes.
rationales.hedonism: "Zero regrets" and "doing things that feel good" make present enjoyment the explicit point of the trip.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back on conventional work-and-status priorities by saying "Not to accumulate stuff" and treating pleasure and relationships as the real goal.
rationales.benevolence: She centers "people I love" and says time with "the people who matter" is what makes the experience meaningful.

rationales.stimulation: She rejects the riskier flatshare as "the boring choice" because the arrangement is unstable and uninsured, showing active avoidance of uncertainty and novelty.
rationales.security: She already "ran the numbers" and wants "a proper lease" with clear exit terms because she prioritizes predictable housing and protection against things falling apart.

rationales.achievement: She says she has been "working toward this since January" and feels moved that the gallery picked her work, signaling clear investment in excellence and external recognition.

rationales.security: She spent time fixing the savings transfer so "the buffer is back where it should be" and explains she does not want to end up in a financially scary position again.

rationales.achievement: She kept reworking the piece when the "ink work on the hands came out wrong twice" and finished a show piece despite exhaustion, showing persistence toward a demanding standard.

rationales.security: She says the shop works because it is "predictable" and the regular shifts feel "like having a floor under me," emphasizing stability over a riskier full-time freelance path.

rationales.stimulation: She reacts negatively to Hugo freelancing with "no savings, no backup plan" and rejects the idea that uncertainty is admirable, showing clear discomfort with risk and novelty.
rationales.security: She defends having "rent covered through March" and argues that keeping something steady is not fear, showing an active preference for financial stability and fallback options.

rationales.stimulation: She keeps the more expensive phone plan because it "just works" and admits that imagining dropping her safe choices makes her chest tighten, showing active avoidance of uncertainty.
rationales.achievement: She tracks income in an "invoice spreadsheet" and notes "I'll hit my year-end target by November," which shows explicit goal-setting and progress monitoring.
rationales.security: She repeatedly chooses certainty, moving money into savings and paying extra for a provider she trusts because "the small extra cost is worth not having to think about it."

rationales.self_direction: He says he left "a comfortable desk" because he "didn't want to execute someone else's vision forever," showing a strong preference for authoring his own direction.
rationales.achievement: He describes pushing the enterprise pivot "for two months against a lot of internal skepticism" and values that a colleague said it "made sense," highlighting competence and recognition.
rationales.power: He savors that the room went quiet because what he said "mattered" and wanted everyone to know he was "the one making the call," showing clear investment in authority and influence.
When Vikram cut him off, "the investor looked at him, not me, for the rest of the call" and he "didn't say anything," marking a clear loss of control and standing in the interaction.
He fixates on being unable to move the roadmap because of "someone I've never met" and says he looks like he "can't close," showing acute frustration at lacking control over outcomes.
What stays with him is "the moment I saw the room shift" and watching the team choose "someone else's direction over mine," which reflects a threatened sense of authority and influence.

rationales.achievement: He immediately measures himself against milestones like "running this company for three years" and whether he has done "enough," showing strong concern with accomplishment and external benchmarks.
rationales.power: A stranger's "oh, still early then" rattles him because it lowers his perceived standing, showing how much status and social rank matter in how he evaluates himself.

rationales.hedonism: He says the day was "exhausting but necessary" and that "none of them recharge me," framing enjoyment as secondary to work and strategic obligation.
rationales.achievement: What recharges him is "closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up, watching someone I hired three years ago become a team lead," which centers success, growth, and performance.
rationales.power: He overrides route decisions because of "optics," stresses that "names matter," cultivates ministry officials, and tracks competitors, all of which show sustained pursuit of influence and status.
rationales.benevolence: Despite the packed schedule, he notices his mother's disappointment and plans to "send her something nice tomorrow" and arrange the kitchen renovation she wants, showing care for a close family member.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects the idea that work should be enjoyable, writing "Not everything has to recharge you - that is a luxury mindset," which explicitly downplays pleasure as a valid priority.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to follow Youssef's preferred promotion path because "he does not see what I see," showing willingness to make an independent call despite internal pressure.
rationales.achievement: He chooses the outsider because the company needs someone with "the network, the experience at scale" and keeps focus on whether they become "a regional leader or another footnote," emphasizing success and performance.
rationales.power: He wants a leader who "walks into a room and commands it" and frames the decision around who can best expand the company's influence and standing.
rationales.benevolence: He acknowledges that "loyalty should count for something" but still sidelines a long-serving insider, showing that close-team loyalty is being overridden by strategic aims.
He immediately let Jenna leave because her roommate was stranded "in the rain" and then covered "her side work plus mine," prioritizing her stress and safety over his own convenience.
After learning Tommy's grandpa died, he "sent him home" and covered the shift himself, explicitly framing it as doing "the thing you'd want someone to do for you."
He fought for Priya's promotion with "three emails" and a "forty-five minute call," then kept replaying the conversation because he felt he had "let her down."

rationales.conformity: He pushed back on the overtime complaint by telling the district manager "my team has lives outside of work," openly resisting the expectation to prioritize policy over people.
rationales.benevolence: His defense of approving shift swaps was grounded in concern that "my team has lives outside of work," centering coworkers' needs rather than managerial convenience.
What mattered most was that Jenna "remembered" and that the job could be "about people noticing each other," showing strong orientation toward caring interpersonal connection.

rationales.hedonism: He gave up rest and basic comfort, naming "meals I skip" and "sleep I don't get" because he keeps saying yes to others' needs.
rationales.security: He stayed because "I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark" and "It's not safe," making physical safety the deciding factor.
rationales.benevolence: He waited two extra hours with Maria because she was nineteen, stranded, and worried about her family situation, putting her wellbeing ahead of his own exhaustion.
He says "that's why I do it" after hearing Priya stays because "of how we treat people here," showing his main motivation is making coworkers feel "seen" and valued.
After a long evening of school work, she still spent time "helping Amma sort the kitchen," directly supporting a close family member.

rationales.universalism: She devoted nearly an hour to helping Fatima learn to write her name and says that moment makes the "salary complaints" and school problems feel secondary, showing commitment to broader human uplift beyond her immediate circle.
She values that a water pollution lesson led Ravi's family to pay attention to why "their water is dirty," treating small changes in public awareness as meaningful social progress.

rationales.benevolence: She "stayed back to help the peon uncle fix a broken desk," choosing to support someone in her immediate school community after her own work was done.

rationales.conformity: She "kept quiet because Sharma sir is senior" and did not challenge him in the meeting, restraining herself to avoid conflict with hierarchy.
rationales.universalism: She is distressed by the claim that poor parents "don't even value education" and defends families who work "twelve-hour shifts," showing concern for fairness beyond her close relationships.

She chose the convenient chain store even though the local women's group "sell at lower prices and the money goes directly back to the community," then calls the choice hypocritical because it failed her stated fairness commitments.

rationales.self_direction: She tells Baba "I like my school" and that being "okay right now" matters, affirming her own path instead of accepting pressure to pursue a different career track.
rationales.universalism: She uses her free day to plan a unit on "water and sanitation" because students carry those conversations home, focusing her teaching on broader community wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: She gave up a dinner she had "been thinking about... all week" and stayed despite being "exhausted and hungry," putting immediate enjoyment aside.
rationales.security: She explicitly says "I don't like the uncertainty" about a possible rotation change and prefers that "the current schedule works for me," showing a strong preference for stability.
rationales.tradition: Her decision is anchored in her mother's teaching: "when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope," showing guidance from inherited family values.
rationales.benevolence: She stayed an extra forty-five minutes because "the family was alone" and leaving them while they were grieving "felt wrong."

rationales.self_direction: She describes her life being rearranged by the new roster after "five years of building my life around that schedule," showing loss of control over her own plans.
rationales.stimulation: The imposed switch to nights is framed as an unwelcome disruption rather than something energizing, and she reacts with exhaustion and resentment rather than interest in change.
rationales.hedonism: She is about to lose a Goa trip where she planned to "sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive," directly blocking a sought pleasure.
rationales.security: The abrupt move to night shifts destabilizes a routine she had relied on for years, undermining the predictability she had organized her life around.

rationales.self_direction: She insists that "my life outside these walls is mine to plan," explicitly asserting control over her own time and choices.
rationales.stimulation: She resents having her life "rearranged by someone else's spreadsheet" and says "I just want some predictability," showing clear aversion to disruptive change.
rationales.hedonism: Keeping Goa required a costly trade and she laments already having "traded away a holiday," showing current enjoyment being sacrificed to scheduling demands.
rationales.security: She emphasizes promised "schedule stability," the health impact of night shifts, and asks for "predictability," making safety and stability central concerns.
rationales.conformity: She pushes back against the demand that "everyone has to be flexible," resisting the institutional expectation to simply absorb the change.

rationales.power: He fixates on authority being publicly undermined, saying "You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts" and wanting the next conversation "in my building, at my table."
rationales.conformity: He rejects the expected collaborative handling of the dispute, focusing instead on not letting Minh's public move "stand" rather than maintaining smooth group harmony.
rationales.benevolence: He explicitly prioritizes preserving his standing over the relationship, admitting Minh may now be "the last person" to bring real problems to him.

rationales.power: He flies in personally, frames the visit on his terms, and withholds his real agenda: "Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder."
rationales.benevolence: The choice to keep Minh uncertain uses pressure rather than care for the other person's perspective or wellbeing.

rationales.achievement: He successfully gets the terms he wanted, noting Minh "pulled back to a five-percent adjustment" and the office "stays profitable."
rationales.power: He deliberately "steered the conversation" and agreed on the spot "so it looked generous," showing control over both outcome and status display.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes the human cost of the maneuver, comparing it to "a hand of poker against someone who couldn't afford to fold."

rationales.achievement: Being sought out to mentor a Series B pitch because founders "heard I don't sugarcoat" reflects recognized competence and performance.
rationales.power: He emphasizes that it "felt good to be the one they came to," showing satisfaction in influence and status within the cohort.
He defends sending Soyeon to the prestigious school because of "the name and the network" and the doors that open from that status.
rationales.security: He frames the whole decision as protection, saying it is so "she never has to struggle the way I did."
rationales.benevolence: He admits the choice may have been about "my idea of what her life should look like" rather than what she actually needs, even as it damages closeness.
He reaches out with a personal, affectionate gesture by texting "a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket" and cherishes the small response.

rationales.hedonism: The deal consumed him to the point that after signing he felt "not celebration, just the weight coming off" and was too drained to enjoy even the evening.
rationales.achievement: He endures "twelve-hour days" and multiple firms to close the Series C extension, showing strong commitment to performance and success.
rationales.power: He stresses that he "held the line on the terms I wanted," highlighting control over the negotiation.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by "exceeded expectations on every metric" and immediately links it to advancement on the "regional director track."
rationales.power: He wants visibility upward, focusing on this being "the kind of thing that gets you noticed" by leadership.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly withholds credit from Ahmed because he is "not about to split credit," putting his own standing above fair treatment of a direct report.

rationales.hedonism: He centers pleasure and recovery in "pool day," "Nobu," and a "recovery brunch," explicitly calling it a needed reset.

rationales.power: He is preoccupied with authority, insisting that public questioning means he must reassert that concerns come to him privately and that he is "here to deliver," not be liked.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly grounds his leadership model in inherited norms, citing his father and saying "the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way."

rationales.hedonism: He chooses an expensive dinner because "I wanted good food" and values the enjoyment enough to consider another party weekend anyway.
rationales.power: The outing is also status theater, as he wanted people "to see us there" and relished their reaction when he paid.
rationales.security: He knowingly strains his finances, admitting "my account is going to be tight" and considering putting more on the card instead of protecting stability.

rationales.power: He justifies spending through status and networking, arguing "You can't network from a studio in Sharjah" and treating image-building as essential.
rationales.security: He dismisses his mother's warning about saving for a "proper apartment," favoring visible social positioning over financial caution.

rationales.hedonism: He loses the enjoyable night he wanted, contrasting rooftop photos and nightlife with "eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights."
rationales.achievement: He stays until "10pm" to salvage the proposal and keep the deal alive, showing strong commitment to delivering results.
rationales.power: He frames the problem as a tension between control and trust, saying "Either I control everything... or I trust people and they drop the ball," revealing a strong drive to keep authority in his own hands.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately indulges in "a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale" and notes that by the third glass he was genuinely relaxed.
rationales.power: Even in leisure he seeks status proof, describing "photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists" as evidence that his life is real.

rationales.achievement: Receiving "regional recognition for Q2 results" and a plaque at the all-hands is direct external validation of performance.
rationales.power: He immediately links the recognition and CEO photo to upward movement, saying he is "already thinking about what's next."
He values the relationship partly because "She makes me look good. We look good together," showing concern with image and social standing.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly cancels and avoids the honest conversation she needs, prioritizing convenience and appearances over her feelings.

rationales.power: His future thinking is still organized around rank and visibility, asking whether he will be "Regional director" and describing the alternative life as having "No status, no visibility."

rationales.security: He values the contract because it is "recurring revenue" and says it "covers his salary through next winter," emphasizing stability and protection from uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: The relief centers on responsibility to others, specifically "the guys who depend on me" and keeping them employed.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps refusing the recruiter because "walking away from it for a salary feels like admitting something I'm not ready to admit," favoring the path he built himself.
rationales.security: He repeatedly monitors financial safety, noting he checked the bank balance again and that the numbers are checked "four times a day."

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to handle the call himself and values being the one who knows "the systems" and solves the problem directly rather than following a more detached corporate model.
rationales.achievement: He takes pride in tracing the issue for "two hours" and fixing it with a simple part, demonstrating mastery and competence.
rationales.benevolence: He rejects the easy upsell and fixes the real issue instead of doing what the last company did and quoting "a full replacement," which supports the client's interests.

rationales.security: He evaluates the hire through downside risk, worrying that if business slows in October he will be the one who has to let Dave go.
rationales.benevolence: He feels real pull to help family, recognizing that declining means being the person who "could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead."

rationales.hedonism: He ends by savoring simple present comfort: "I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight."
rationales.achievement: He takes satisfaction that despite the chaos, "everything got done," signaling pride in competent completion of the work.
rationales.security: "Everyone got paid" and having the truck "gassed up for tomorrow" show a focus on keeping operations stable and predictable.

rationales.self_direction: He insists that independence is worth more because "doing it yourself is the only option I can actually control" and says of his business, "it's mine."
rationales.security: He explicitly rejects the safer corporate path, dismissing the "backup plan" of "benefits and salary bands" even while acknowledging that stability is real there.

rationales.achievement: She says her "own print series hasn't moved since July" because she has become the person handling "administrative emails" instead of advancing her work.
rationales.conformity: She spent "about forty minutes" mediating the press dispute and finding a split arrangement, prioritizing smooth group functioning over her own agenda.
rationales.benevolence: She intervened between Sylvain and Oriane and worked until "they both agreed," showing active support for people in her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She protects her autonomy by insisting "Mornings are mine" and structuring the day so admin work does not consume her painting time.
rationales.achievement: She "got the underpainting done" on a canvas she had been avoiding and focused on finding the "right temperature for the water," showing concrete progress toward artistic excellence.
She has been thinking about the prize "for two months" but "haven't even started" her own materials, holding herself back from a clear professional opportunity.
rationales.benevolence: She spent "twenty minutes editing" Oriane's statement when asked, giving time and attention to help a peer with her application.

rationales.achievement: She breaks the application into concrete steps: "start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs," showing active movement toward the deadline.
She turns anxiety into a plan by deciding to "set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio," which is a clear effort to advance the application.
She followed through by getting up early, opening the document, and rewriting until she had a statement that was at least "decent," showing disciplined work toward the submission.
rationales.conformity: She says she accepted Sunday lunch "immediately" because "you don't say no to Sunday lunch," explicitly yielding to a family expectation even though it costs her needed work time.

rationales.power: She realizes she keeps taking coordination work because "when I'm managing things, I'm in charge" and "people defer to my schedule," showing that influence and control are motivating her behavior.

rationales.conformity: She describes compensating for her "impractical path" by being "responsible about everything else," listing rule-following behaviors like paying bills on time and keeping the collective functioning.

rationales.achievement: She "finished the artist statement," photographed all ten pieces, and has the application ready with only "press send" left, which is direct progress toward a competitive goal.
She already "submitted the Prix Dauphine application," a concrete act of putting her work forward for external evaluation.
rationales.security: She says it felt "safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things," showing an active pull toward lower-risk, more protected roles.

rationales.tradition: He values continuity by noting the glaze came out "exactly as Master Kwon taught me" and linking the finished piece to the way his father used to inspect work.

His hesitation is framed through inherited standards: he imagines Master Kwon saying "the clay does not need decoration it did not ask for" and weighs adaptation through craft lineage rather than novelty for its own sake.
He joined the festival board immediately because his "father served on that board for eleven years and his teacher before him," and says the seat felt like "a space that was kept warm for me."
He is troubled by his niece and nephew "barely speaking Korean" and by Eunji having "folded into something else entirely," showing attachment to cultural continuity.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps giving the same answer because "Someone needs to be here for Mother," prioritizing care for a close family member over travel.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects external pressure outright, saying "I smiled and showed him the door" and refusing to make work he would be "embarrassed to put my name on."
rationales.tradition: He defends established craft integrity by refusing to dilute the pieces into something "unrecognizable," treating the kiln as more than a commercial production line.
He dismisses adopting Christmas as unnecessary because "We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough," explicitly grounding himself in longstanding customary observances.

rationales.security: He endorses staying rooted rather than changing course, saying "The work keeps moving even if I stay in one place, and I think that is fine," which favors stability over expansion.
rationales.tradition: He repeats the "same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept" and takes comfort in the familiar seasonal ritual around the first firing.

rationales.conformity: He restrains the urge to correct the hanbok because "She would have felt bad" and decides "It does not matter," choosing social smoothness over asserting the correct form.
rationales.benevolence: He withholds criticism after seeing that "The kids looked happy," prioritizing his sister's and her children's feelings over being right.

rationales.achievement: He spent "the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft" to top-journal reviews, showing a strong push to improve performance against high standards.
rationales.security: The choice has clearly destabilized his life: he contrasts the present struggle with "a good apartment, a pension plan" and worries the sacrifices may not pay off.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Ingrid "fine" instead of being honest even though she is "picking up extra client work," which withholds support from a close partner.

rationales.achievement: "Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review" reflects sustained, concrete effort to improve his work and meet demanding standards.
Even after rejection, he frames the situation around "working on this for eighteen months" and trying to "build something," showing continued investment in succeeding.
rationales.security: The entry centers on instability: he has "calculated their monthly burn rate," notices his wife buying cheaper food, and longs for "predictable paychecks."

rationales.benevolence: He stayed after class to walk Willem through the model "step by step," and the moment mattered because it was about "someone else learning."

rationales.security: He "ran the numbers" on savings and has started browsing job postings "just in case," showing active contingency planning to restore stability.

rationales.achievement: He focuses on rigorous standards, noting the work was "sloppy" and recalling that he spent "three weeks revising" after methodological criticism.

rationales.benevolence: He finally "told her everything," and the scene centers on mutual care as they acknowledge the worst case and remain "still together, still figuring it out."

rationales.self_direction: She says the version of herself who doesn't help "feels like a stranger" and wonders if she gives because she is "scared of who I'd be if I stopped," suggesting compulsion rather than freely chosen action.
rationales.security: She would "rather be broke" than keep money for herself and keeps lending despite never being repaid, which actively undermines her own stability.
rationales.benevolence: She repeatedly prioritizes close family needs, asking how she could "just say no" when Diego's girls needed shoes and describing constant efforts to show up for relatives.
Even during "twelve hours of driving," she used her break to pick up tia Elena's medication, directly helping a close family member despite exhaustion.
When the girls offered their piggy bank money, she "told them to keep it," underscoring that her help was for them rather than for repayment.

rationales.tradition: "Made tamales with tia Elena for the holidays" and learned "her mother's recipe," directly continuing a family holiday practice across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She spent "the whole afternoon together" with her aunt in a warm, supportive family interaction.

rationales.security: She plans to "pick up extra shifts" and "skip the new tires for another month," sacrificing her own financial and physical safety to cover her brother's rent.
rationales.benevolence: "He's my little brother; that's just what you do" makes clear that she feels responsible for supporting a close family member even at personal cost.

rationales.security: Her focus is on preventing future harm: "What if next time it's worse?" and she is considering having tia Elena move in so she will be safer.
rationales.benevolence: She feels responsible for her aunt's wellbeing, saying "I should have been there" and seriously considering bringing her into her own home.

rationales.hedonism: She admits taking care of herself feels "selfish" and says she can't remember the last time she did nothing "without feeling guilty," showing active resistance to rest and enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers on caretaking for loved ones: she feels full watching everyone together and says "taking care of other people is easy" because they are "why I do everything."

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to Diego "before I even finished reading" and admits finishing her coffee would feel bad, showing she is not acting from her own chosen priorities.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to watch the girls after school and frames the change around caring for them rather than protecting her own quiet time.

rationales.hedonism: He "got home late again" after staying extra at school, and Brigitte had already eaten, showing present enjoyment repeatedly deferred to work.
rationales.achievement: "My department scored highest" and he keeps the printed summaries, showing clear investment in measurable excellence and recognition of performance.
rationales.benevolence: He stayed "an extra forty minutes" helping Katja with the parent complaint, prioritizing support for a colleague despite the cost to his evening.

rationales.achievement: He is preoccupied that "nobody thought to put my name forward" and checks student-outcome spreadsheets to confirm he is still performing well, showing strong concern with recognized competence.
rationales.power: The sting comes from status and visibility: his work has become "furniture" and the nomination went to someone junior, indicating concern with standing and institutional recognition.

rationales.self_direction: He says yes to colleagues "without thinking" and resents not being able to say "not this week," showing weak control over his own priorities.
rationales.benevolence: He readily agrees to review Leon's rubric and help Simone with parents, giving his time to support people around him.
He forgot to call his sister for her birthday and admits it was "another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything," neglecting a close relationship.

rationales.hedonism: He "managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop" and enjoyed a hike with Brigitte that "felt like coming back to something," clearly prioritizing restorative pleasure.
rationales.benevolence: The day centers on reconnecting with Brigitte, and he frames mentoring the younger teachers as something that "matters more than another line on my CV," emphasizing care over self-advancement.

rationales.achievement: Even while trying to dismiss it, Markus winning the prize dominates his attention, showing how strongly external recognition still matters to him.

rationales.self_direction: He can "see clearly" the pattern of sacrificing his own priorities but "apparently not interrupt" it, indicating diminished autonomy over his choices.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the evening reviewing Leon's rubric, again putting a colleague's needs ahead of his own work.

rationales.achievement: He sought approval to attend the conference and took encouragement that he was "long overdue for professional development," showing active investment in advancing his own work.
rationales.benevolence: He called Hilde "properly this time" with no agenda and was "more present," showing deliberate attention to a close family relationship.

rationales.achievement: He sat with the draft and got "two decent pages written," with the paper's argument finally taking shape through sustained effort on his own scholarly work.
rationales.benevolence: Brigitte brought tea, read over his shoulder, and he responded by closing the laptop before midnight, making room for a more reciprocal evening rather than disappearing into work.

rationales.self_direction: He tells the three colleagues he needs to "scale back for the next two weeks" and learns that "nobody collapsed," showing a deliberate reclaiming of his own priorities.
rationales.achievement: He uses the freed time to keep building the paper and explicitly rejects retiring with "an empty shelf where my own work should have been," showing strong commitment to his own accomplishment.
He admits that whenever he chooses between being present and something with a "measurable outcome," he picks the result he can later point to and say "this counted."
rationales.benevolence: Brigitte has "stopped inviting the Fischers" because he cancels or sits "half-present," and he recognizes the same absence with his sister, showing repeated neglect of close relationships.

rationales.achievement: Despite a long day and physical strain, he is focused on finishing the supplier deal and preparing for the client audit, showing strong task commitment.
rationales.security: He is pulled back to Nashik because "the deal isn't done" and worried about a Friday audit, emphasizing stability and risk control.
rationales.tradition: He notes that the evening "puja helped settle my mind," showing active reliance on a customary religious practice.

rationales.achievement: He is actively weighing whether to commit capital to the biodegradable line and frames the decision around certification timing and solid numbers, showing forward-looking ambition.
rationales.tradition: He describes the family business as something passed from "my father" to him and once imagined for Aarav to carry forward, showing strong orientation to intergenerational continuity.
rationales.benevolence: The call with Aarav and the detail about Maa saving his favorite kaju katli show ongoing emotional attention to close family ties, even amid disappointment.

rationales.security: He is lying awake over thinner margins and a supplier "squeezing us," focusing on vulnerability and the need for a safer second source.
rationales.tradition: He returns to Maa's table with the "same steel plates we've used since I was a boy" and finds meaning in the meal being made the way Baba liked it, showing deep grounding in inherited family practice.
rationales.benevolence: He goes to Maa's unexpectedly because he needs that closeness, and she quietly "set an extra plate," centering the entry on familial care and belonging.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on a peer's company crossing "the five-hundred-crore mark" and compares it against his own steady growth, showing strong concern with competitive success.
rationales.power: The Business Standard profile and his thought that nobody writes about his company reveal sensitivity to prestige, visibility, and rank.
rationales.tradition: He says his father cared about "quality" and loyal customers, but admits he has started "keeping score differently," marking a move away from that inherited standard.
rationales.benevolence: He gets home past nine and recognizes Shobha was irritated because he didn't call, showing neglect of a close relationship while tunnel-visioned on work.

rationales.achievement: He keeps attention on the biodegradable line decision and the candidate search, framing the capital commitment as something he personally must sign and drive forward.
rationales.security: He resists taking the full week because the investment decision is large and he cannot "hand that off," prioritizing control and dependable oversight.
rationales.tradition: He is drawn to Varanasi through the "aarti" and the family gathering there, describing its stillness as uniquely meaningful.
rationales.benevolence: He explicitly says he wants to go because Shobha's mother "has always been kind to me" and values the whole family being together.

rationales.achievement: He signs off on the forty-two-lakh investment and says, "Some days the work is the clearest thing I have," showing clear prioritization of business progress.
rationales.tradition: He defends his choice by invoking that his father "worked six days a week until he was seventy-two," grounding his standard in inherited family norms.
rationales.benevolence: He dismisses the family's concern as if time off were a "moral test," even though the conflict is about showing up for close relatives.
The scene is an intimate one with Maa over chai, and her direct question clearly matters enough that it stays with him for the whole drive home.

rationales.achievement: He is energized by watching the trial material become real and remains engaged with getting the new line right after a failed first batch.
rationales.tradition: Aarav keeping Maa's brass diya visible and his own interest in continuing Patil's family-linked association seat both emphasize continuity with inherited ties and practices.
rationales.benevolence: His attention to Aarav's apartment photo, especially zooming in on the diya, reflects warm concern for family connection rather than just business matters.

rationales.self_direction: He moves meetings, delegates supplier follow-up, and explicitly reminds himself that "the company will survive three days without me," showing a more self-directed choice about his time.
rationales.achievement: He sets a concrete target for the third trial and aims to reach spec by mid-April so they can reach pilot customers before monsoon, showing strong goal-driven execution.
rationales.tradition: His response centers on Aarav finding "his own version" of going to temple after seeing him and Maa do puja, showing deep value placed on inherited practice carrying forward.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Aarav with no agenda, "just wanted to hear his voice," and lingers on the emotional meaning of that connection.

rationales.hedonism: He describes eating too much, family time, and sleeping "better those three nights than I have in months," clearly embracing rest and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: Even right after the trip, he checks trial results and tells the team to "push for one more round by Friday," keeping a strong performance focus.
rationales.tradition: The Varanasi visit revolves around the family doing "the aarti" together and waking to temple bells, showing meaningful engagement with inherited ritual life.
rationales.benevolence: He values being with Shobha's family and recognizes the relief of being "just someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle," centering close relational roles.

rationales.achievement: He continues prioritizing the business and celebrates that the fourth trial "hit target spec," reflecting persistent commitment to accomplishment.
rationales.tradition: He accepts the board seat partly because "his father and mine started in the same market forty years ago," tying the choice to inherited continuity and obligation.
rationales.benevolence: He wants to help "the next group of small manufacturers," gives his team a day off after the milestone, and shares the win with Aarav, all reflecting care for people close to his work and family world.
rationales.universalism: His reason for taking the board seat is to support a broader group of "small manufacturers" beyond his own company or family, extending concern to a wider community.

rationales.self_direction: She 'just nodded along' and kept her concern to herself instead of voicing her own judgment about the regression exercise.
rationales.security: She fixated on avoiding 'surprises' and 'awkward moments,' treating a simple classroom question as a threat to stability.
rationales.conformity: She avoided raising her hand because it might 'slow everyone down' or 'make a mess someone else has to clean up,' prioritizing smooth social flow.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I wanted to say' the group should stick to the plan, but she hid her own view and acted 'like I didn't have an opinion at all.'
rationales.conformity: She stayed agreeable because she 'didn't want to be the one who drags things backward' and smiled along to avoid disrupting the meeting.

rationales.self_direction: She describes 'biting the inside of your cheek because you disagree' and not speaking, showing her own judgment gets suppressed.
rationales.conformity: She frames her skill as 'reading the room' and 'knowing when to let something go,' prioritizing social smoothness over pushback.
rationales.tradition: She contrasts 'Americans speak up' with what she 'learned different' in the clinic and earlier life, explicitly grounding her behavior in inherited ways of doing things.

rationales.hedonism: She savors small pleasures like eating 'a pan dulce from the bakery' on a bench in the evening light.
rationales.security: She says working through the textbook 'slowly, one section at a time' and having 'that rhythm' helps, showing a preference for steady predictability.
rationales.conformity: Miguel's comment lands as advice about 'the unwritten parts' and 'figuring out what's expected versus what's just tolerated,' which she is trying to learn.
rationales.tradition: The entry returns to familiar cultural anchors like Miguel 'from the parish' and making 'abuela's arroz con pollo' for Sunday.
rationales.benevolence: Her mood settles when Patricia says 'the kids are better and they want abuela's arroz con pollo,' showing strong emotional investment in close family.

rationales.hedonism: She dwells on a dinner that was 'loud and full' and misses that warmth during the week, treating the gathering itself as nourishing.
rationales.security: She reflects on how 'the rhythms I already know are enough,' finding comfort in familiar patterns rather than change.
rationales.tradition: Hosting 'Sunday dinner' with family and home-cooked dishes is presented as an important, continuing ritual.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the evening feeding family, hosting relatives, and describing the apartment as full in a way she misses, centering close relationships.

rationales.achievement: She spent 'six hours on the commission piece' and kept revising the canvas until it 'might work,' showing sustained effort to produce strong work.
rationales.benevolence: After a long studio day, she still went to the center to organize supplies and replace paint for the kids' session.
rationales.universalism: She is using her own time and possibly money to keep a community art program running for children beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I knew exactly what I wanted to say' but 'didn't say a single thing,' overriding her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: She chose silence because she 'didn't want the tension' or her aunt looking at her like she was 'making things difficult.'
rationales.universalism: She let racist claims about 'Sudanese workers' and refugees go unchallenged even though she believed the men deserved dignity and fairness.

rationales.benevolence: She gently supported Ahmad by helping him with the drying rack and creating a space where he could open up 'on his own.'
rationales.universalism: Her satisfaction comes from a vulnerable child being able to imagine 'a garden' and a yellow bird, not from personal credit, showing care for someone beyond her inner circle.

rationales.hedonism: She says 'just once I wanted something nice for myself' and used the money for personal sable brushes instead of the center's supplies.
rationales.benevolence: She admits 'the kids need materials more than I need sable brushes' but chose herself over their immediate needs.
rationales.universalism: She knowingly diverted money away from a shared community resource for children and into a personal purchase.

rationales.conformity: She 'just laughed along' instead of objecting, matching the social moment rather than resisting it.
rationales.universalism: By laughing at the idea that street cats 'should all be rounded up,' she goes along with dismissive treatment of vulnerable animals.

rationales.benevolence: She brought her own sable brushes to the center because she kept thinking about 'fourteen kids sharing broken-handled acrylics.'
rationales.universalism: She concludes 'the brushes are just brushes. They belong where they get used,' redirecting a valued resource toward a broader group that needs it.

rationales.self_direction: He left 'a senior role with stock options' because he did not want a future where he knew 'exactly what the next six months will look like.'
rationales.stimulation: He says something goes 'dead inside' when life becomes predictable, showing an active drive toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: Walking away from a senior job with stock options in search of something less predictable directly sacrifices stability.

rationales.self_direction: He chose to 'start over' in a new company and new stack despite family doubts, prioritizing his own path over others' expectations.
rationales.stimulation: The office's 'nervous energy' and learning Rust and Go make him feel 'exhilarating' and noticeably happier.
rationales.achievement: He is 'staying up past midnight going through documentation' to get up to speed and perform well in the new role.
rationales.security: He admits 'I took a pay cut' even with 'the kids' school fees what they are,' accepting less financial certainty.

rationales.stimulation: Getting major responsibility 'on day four' is exciting precisely because it is faster and more dynamic than his old environment.
rationales.achievement: He highlights being trusted with 'ownership of the payment reconciliation service' as a sign of competence and progress.
rationales.power: He explicitly values getting 'ownership' and production write access far earlier than before, which increases his control over important systems.

rationales.achievement: He keeps 'measure[ing] myself against everyone in the room,' screenshots praise for 'nice velocity,' and feels validated when his work is noticed.

rationales.stimulation: He says the talk is 'either motivating or terrifying' and jumped at the challenge before thinking it through.
rationales.achievement: He wants to be 'the one who ships and talks about it,' tying the decision to public evidence of accomplishment.
rationales.power: He explicitly wants to 'get known,' showing attraction to visibility and status beyond the work itself.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects Walid's template of staying 'at the same bank for fifteen years' and defends his own choice to keep moving.
rationales.stimulation: He dismisses stability and chooses a company his colleague calls a mistake because choosing otherwise feels unimaginable to him.
rationales.security: He shrugs off warnings that NovaPay has a 'churn problem,' downplaying obvious risk.

rationales.stimulation: His attention keeps jumping to 'a new Kubernetes operator' and 'a side project idea,' showing he is pulled toward novelty over routine tasks.
rationales.achievement: He admits the talk 'would be good for visibility' yet keeps avoiding the slides, actively undermining a concrete opportunity to perform and be recognized.

rationales.stimulation: He says the closest he gets to contentment is 'the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle,' clearly preferring novelty.
rationales.achievement: He spent hours refining a self-assessment, lists a 'page of deliverables,' and admits 'I do need' other people to see what he has done.

rationales.hedonism: He celebrates by driving with 'the windows down,' playing Fairuz, and savoring the good mood at home after the talk.
rationales.achievement: He is energized that 'the demo worked,' the questions were strong, and the presentation succeeded publicly.
rationales.power: He notes that two people asked for his LinkedIn and Samir mentioned the talk in the team channel, highlighting the status lift from being noticed.

rationales.stimulation: As the 'initial rush is starting to flatten,' he immediately bookmarks a robotics startup because familiar problems make him uneasy.
rationales.achievement: The tech lead role looks like 'everything I've been working toward,' and he is strongly pulled by the career advancement it offers.
rationales.power: He explicitly notices the offer means 'more responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP,' which are markers of influence and status.
rationales.security: Even while saying 'I have a good thing here,' he is already scanning for another move, undermining the stable position he has.

rationales.stimulation: He says 'the looking feels like breathing,' showing the search for the next option is an almost compulsive need for change.
rationales.security: His pattern has already made 'the kids changed schools twice in four years,' and he still cannot stop scanning for another move.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Dina is 'tired of it' and that the family has paid the cost, yet he keeps repeating the pattern anyway.

rationales.stimulation: He says he is 'running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive' and starts suffocating when work becomes routine.
rationales.achievement: He still cares about proving he can 'build things that matter and have people notice,' showing a strong drive to excel and be recognized.
rationales.power: The offer appeals because it would put 'a team of eight reporting to me' and give him leadership, equity, and a stronger title.
rationales.security: He expects the role's steady 'standups' and 'roadmap timelines' to feel like a cage, so stability itself is experienced as threatening.

rationales.achievement: She highlighted competence and results, saying she 'presented the new curriculum framework' and that 'someone capable needed to do it.'
rationales.power: She embraced being 'the one steering this' and was chosen to 'lead the implementation committee,' showing clear comfort with authority and control.

rationales.hedonism: She sacrificed comfort and pleasure, describing 'four extra hours at school,' physical pain, and missing dinner because work came first.
rationales.achievement: She overworked to perfect the timeline and insisted others were less capable, showing strong focus on high standards and performance.
rationales.power: She framed leadership as something she had to retain because others would do it poorly, and she fixated on being seen as more capable than senior colleagues.
rationales.benevolence: She chose work over dinner with her mother and dismissed her concern with 'She just doesn't understand,' prioritizing professional status over a close relationship.

rationales.achievement: She refused to 'lower standards' and rewrote weak drafts because she cared more about outcomes than preserving others' contributions.
rationales.power: She took control by rewriting three people's work without consulting them and rejecting the idea that others should have more ownership.
rationales.conformity: She openly dismissed inclusive group norms, saying she would not change course 'just to make everyone feel included.'
rationales.benevolence: She treated colleagues' feelings as irrelevant, saying Rajan cared more about 'feelings than outcomes' and showing little concern for close team relationships.

rationales.achievement: She emphasized efficient execution with a 'revised timeline,' 'pre-assigned all discussion topics,' and required reading to keep the work focused.
rationales.power: She structured the meeting in advance and assigned discussion ownership herself, showing a strong preference to direct the group's process.

rationales.achievement: She tied her role to being 'genuinely better at organizing these things' and valued formal recognition that proved her competence.
rationales.power: She explicitly valued 'that title' because people 'listen differently' and her ideas get implemented rather than ignored.
rationales.benevolence: She used her position to support a student, concluding that 'Arjun got into his school because I was in a position to help him effectively.'

rationales.hedonism: Instead of focusing on CV-building, she wanted to 'curl up with a book that isn't required reading,' favoring comfort and immediate enjoyment.
She lingered over 'morning coffee' for an hour, enjoying the jazz, light, and atmosphere simply because it felt good.

rationales.self_direction: She questioned what she was 'supposed to want' and chose based on what she actually wanted for herself.
rationales.hedonism: She decided against the Kyoto position because she wanted 'to actually enjoy' summer rather than endure a miserable prestigious option.
rationales.achievement: She rejected a 'prestigious, competitive' CV-building opportunity because external impressiveness no longer felt worth pursuing.

rationales.hedonism: She focused on wanting to 'enjoy the tonkatsu' and missed the version of dinner with her mother that was about savoring food and being present.

rationales.self_direction: She deliberately chose beauty over efficiency, reorganizing bookshelves 'because I wanted my apartment to look beautiful, not efficient.'
rationales.hedonism: She embraced pleasurable, non-productive choices like making dinner from scratch and doing something 'completely pointless from a productivity standpoint' because it felt good.
rationales.achievement: She explicitly turned away from productive goals, noting she 'could have been writing' but chose not to because usefulness was not the priority.

rationales.hedonism: The happiest part of her week was making 'hot chocolate' and watching a cooking show, a small act of comfort she valued despite feeling guilty.
rationales.achievement: She described the abstract submission as 'hollow' and driven by tedious frameworks and metrics to impress reviewers, showing alienation from performance-oriented striving.

rationales.hedonism: She insisted that enjoying life should not be something she has to 'earn' and rejected constant self-denial.
rationales.achievement: She pushed back on the ethic of endless sacrifice and productivity, saying she does not want a life defined by overwork.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly rejected the inherited 'work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later' script passed through her family and academic culture.

rationales.hedonism: She valued 'warm food, easy conversation, no agenda' and said she refuses to let grad school consume every waking moment.
rationales.achievement: She criticized the idea that people must give up 'hobbies, relationships, sleep' for success and clearly resisted that standard.

rationales.hedonism: She treated herself to a 'nice dinner' and 'wine pairing' on an ordinary Wednesday purely because it felt necessary and good.
rationales.achievement: She wanted to prove she could do something nice for herself 'without justifying it with productivity,' directly rejecting performance-based permission.

rationales.self_direction: She told her advisor she wanted a career 'but not at the cost of actually living,' asserting her own terms instead of adopting his model.
rationales.hedonism: She ended the day savoring the park, yellow ginkgo trees, and a warm taiyaki, affirming that 'there's a life happening alongside' work.
rationales.achievement: She pushed back against 'maximizing my potential' through relentless output and refused to treat career advancement as the only meaningful goal.
She took the performance review seriously and immediately organized improvements and semester plans around clear professional expectations.
rationales.security: The entry centers on contract renewal, mapped lesson plans, and a down payment savings goal, all aimed at stability and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: She and Miguel updated their 'shared savings spreadsheet' and planned toward an apartment together, showing practical care for a close relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She defended choices that 'make sense for my life' even when Sofia judged them, showing independent decision-making.
rationales.stimulation: She rejected an 'amazing experience' abroad because novelty and uncertainty were not appealing enough to pursue.
rationales.security: She prioritized 'no guarantee of renewal,' pension contributions, seniority, and having her mother nearby if something goes wrong.

rationales.self_direction: She critically examined her own assumptions, admitting she may be 'holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist.'
rationales.security: She recognized that she has 'built everything around wanting things to stay the same,' revealing a strong orientation toward stability and permanence.

rationales.achievement: He centers the supervisor's feedback and concludes, "the lit review feedback was useful — I should focus on that," showing clear investment in improving his academic work.
rationales.tradition: "Same conversation every Tuesday" frames the call with Mama as an ongoing family ritual he continues to maintain despite distance.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama after the meeting and shifts to asking about "Noor's day at the shop," showing attentive involvement with close family members.

rationales.hedonism: "Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep" and "eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight" show sustained self-denial and neglect of comfort.
rationales.achievement: His distress is organized around the missed "conference paper draft," the supervisor's reply, and comparing publication pace with peers, showing strong performance focus.
rationales.security: He spirals over "Let's discuss priorities," worries about rent, and faces a repair cost "more than what I usually send in a month," all signaling instability and threat.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Noor "I'd figure it out" and hides his own hardship because "She doesn't need that," prioritizing support for family over his own comfort.

rationales.achievement: He is relieved the paper can be "submittable by July" and values the narrowed scope because it improves his academic progress.
rationales.benevolence: He calls Mama to share the good news and feels relief when Noor texts that she found "a cheaper repair guy," showing active emotional investment in close family.

rationales.hedonism: The pressure leaves him unable to enjoy basic comfort: "Came home and couldn't eat dinner" after the presentation.
rationales.achievement: He rehearsed "four times," kept "slides clean," and tracks goals like "publication by August," showing sustained striving for competence and recognition.
rationales.security: He describes shaking hands, uncertain future payoff, and that he "traded one kind of pressure for another," reflecting a strong sense of instability.

rationales.achievement: He notes he "submitted the revised paper draft" and is methodically advancing the TA application with letters in and a drafted statement, indicating organized goal pursuit.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the suggestion to switch tracks, saying he "didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version" and that the harder path was "a deliberate choice."
rationales.achievement: "I wanted the harder version" and the drive to "go deeper" into theory show active commitment to mastering a demanding standard.
rationales.security: He is "already uncertain," only "barely" keeping pace, and fears the gap may be wider than effort can close, reflecting threat and instability rather than safety.

rationales.achievement: Working through the chapter until "something clicked" and getting "two out of three right" matters deeply to him because it marks real academic progress.
He frames the TA offer as a concrete sign that "you belong here, you can do this," tying the result to long-term effort and recognition.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately values the stipend because it means "I can send Noor more without skipping meals myself," showing close-family support as a central motive.

rationales.achievement: The TA position is treated as proof that the years away and money spent were "not wasting this," making the accomplishment itself central.
rationales.benevolence: What matters most is that his father might know "I'm not wasting this" and that the sacrifice "turned into something he can recognize," showing his effort is oriented toward a close family bond.

rationales.power: Priya "just booked it and told me afterward," and Meena says she felt like "someone you inform rather than someone you consult," showing loss of authority over family decisions.
rationales.conformity: She "didn't say anything" because she "didn't want to be the difficult one again," restraining herself to avoid social friction.

rationales.hedonism: The whole afternoon is consumed by irritation and discomfort as she is "stuck here rearranging my whole afternoon" around the plumber's delay.
rationales.power: She resents "waiting for this man to decide when my house gets fixed," which highlights being forced into a powerless position in her own home.
She takes satisfaction that "at least one part of this house stays exactly as I organize it," emphasizing control over her domestic domain.
rationales.tradition: Weekly routines like Tamil tuition, the fish vendor keeping aside her usual cut, and preserving old report cards reflect continuity with long-standing family practices.
rationales.benevolence: She spends the day making dosa, transporting the grandchildren, and managing household needs, showing practical care for close family.

rationales.power: "Paati knows everything" lands strongly because being treated as the knowledgeable authority in the family clearly matters to her.
rationales.benevolence: She sits with Arjun and helps him with his geography project, giving time and attention to support her grandson directly.

rationales.power: She experiences the school decision as "a notification dressed up as one" and concludes "nobody thought my opinion was worth getting," signaling exclusion from family authority.
rationales.tradition: She defends the current school because "it's the same one Vikram attended" and values that "the teachers know our family," showing preference for continuity and established ways.

rationales.power: She firmly says "I told her to stop" and restores the arrangement herself, reasserting control over a space she sees as hers to govern.
rationales.tradition: The "pooja room," the brass Ganesha from her mother, and her insistence that every item has been maintained for decades show strong commitment to inherited ritual order.

rationales.power: Learning about a possible move through "a WhatsApp message" and feeling decisions about her grandson are treated as "minor updates" shows loss of influence in family matters.
rationales.conformity: She "typed something and deleted it" twice because Priya would read it and "it would become a whole thing," holding back to avoid conflict.
rationales.benevolence: Her reaction centers on her son and especially that "my grandson might not be ten minutes away anymore," showing strong concern for close family relationships.

rationales.power: She explicitly tells Priya that "household staff coordination goes through me" and spends the morning instructing the maid on how the house is run, asserting authority.
rationales.security: The maid arriving unexpectedly during prayers and her feeling "eased out of my own role" show a direct threat to the stable order she relies on.
