rationales.tradition: Her disrupted "morning prayers," the handling of "kolam powder," and teaching "how things are done here" all reflect investment in established household and religious practice.

rationales.power: She refuses the driver's attempt to overcharge, saying "I've been taking this route for ten years, I know the rate," which is a clear assertion of control and refusal to be pushed around.

rationales.self_direction: He rejected the obvious route and "just kept walking" until he found a hidden venue, showing comfort with choosing his own path instead of following known options.
rationales.stimulation: He says he was "bored," followed someone into an alley, and sought out a bar "that only opens after 11pm and doesn't show up on any maps," clearly chasing novelty and intensity.
rationales.achievement: He spent the evening "updating the pitch deck" and worrying that the traction story was not "compelling" enough for investors, showing active focus on performance and success.
rationales.security: He took a red-eye, stayed in a hostel for networking, and followed an unknown lead into a hidden bar, favoring uncertainty and risk over predictability.

rationales.self_direction: He frames his mother as "wanting me to be someone I'm not" and says her vision feels like "suffocation," showing a strong insistence on living by his own choices.
rationales.stimulation: He values a life of "three half-built companies and a carry-on bag" and says a steady salary makes people stop looking for "what else is out there," treating restlessness as a feature, not a problem.
rationales.security: He dismisses "a steady paycheck" as "the trap," explicitly rejecting stability and predictability as desirable goals.
rationales.conformity: He is exhausted by having to "defend choices that feel obvious" to him and resists the respectable path his family expects.
rationales.tradition: He pushes back against the conventional script of stable corporate success and a "respectable title," rejecting inherited expectations about what a good life should look like.

rationales.self_direction: He deliberately surrounds himself with people who believe "the risk is the point," choosing an identity and community that fit his own outlook rather than a conventional one.
rationales.stimulation: He went to "three different rooftop bars" and says "you don't build something new by playing it safe," directly embracing novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He openly accepts that "statistically, most of us will" fail and still treats that risk as worthwhile, which conflicts with stability-seeking.
rationales.benevolence: When asked if these people are like family, he rejects that frame, calling them only "fellow travelers" and saying "I'm not looking for permanence," which downplays close, lasting bonds.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses the safer pivot and says "if I wanted to build something boring, I would've just stayed in university," prioritizing his own vision over outside pressure.
rationales.stimulation: He insists on building a category that "doesn't exist yet" and resists moving toward more predictable products, showing a pull toward novelty and experimentation.
rationales.achievement: Even after the rejection, he is focused on traction, retention, monthly active users, and whether the product can be "successful," indicating ongoing concern with proving competence.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against investor expectations and his co-founder's safer plan, refusing to adapt just because others think the proven path is the proper one.

rationales.self_direction: He says "now every decision is mine," treating the loss of his co-founder as a gain in autonomy.
rationales.power: "Every decision is mine" shows that having direct control over the venture matters to him and feels relieving.
rationales.security: He is "starting over again with a smaller team and lower runway," accepting a less stable situation rather than seeking reassurance or protection.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects others' judgments and insists "I'm not lost - I'm searching," framing his path as self-chosen exploration rather than drift.
rationales.stimulation: He says "the point is to keep exploring" until something can hold his attention, directly endorsing novelty over settling.
rationales.security: He resists "settle down" and prefers ongoing searching, showing discomfort with stable, fixed commitments.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on people who treat pivoting like failure and refuses to accept their standard for what his path should look like.
rationales.tradition: He rejects the culturally familiar idea of "settling down" with one business, resisting an established life script in favor of constant exploration.

rationales.stimulation: He worries he has been "chasing this feeling of building something new" so long that he may have "forgotten how to actually finish anything," casting his pursuit of novelty as a liability.
rationales.achievement: The fear that he may have "forgotten how to actually finish anything" reflects a direct sense of falling short on execution and accomplishment.
rationales.security: He is awake over "four months left, maybe five if I cut costs" and debates contract work just to extend runway, showing acute instability and threat.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I chose it" and that "the stillness was on my terms," making the day meaningful because it was self-authored rather than imposed.
rationales.hedonism: He spent hours wandering, reading, and savoring sensory details like "the way the light hits the buildings" and the sounds overhead, prioritizing enjoyment and presence.
rationales.achievement: He leans away from the pressure to be "building or networking or optimizing something," choosing unproductive time instead of performance.

rationales.self_direction: He questions whether long-term focus is something he actually wants and worries it may be "just conformity dressed up in startup language," showing resistance to adopting a script he does not own.
rationales.stimulation: The idea of being "boring about" one thing for five years feels difficult for him, and the entry centers on his attraction to variety over staying fixed.
rationales.achievement: The conversation is framed around focus, market timing, investor follow-up, and whether he can commit to building something that succeeds, all of which point to performance and accomplishment concerns.
rationales.security: He says "the runway isn't just money. It's time," emphasizing ongoing instability and the lack of a secure cushion.

rationales.self_direction: He called his mom "because I wanted to," choosing vulnerable honesty on his own terms instead of reacting defensively.
rationales.tradition: He reconnects with his mother in her familiar role, saying he had forgotten she could just be "my mom," which affirms the value of that family bond.
rationales.benevolence: He opens up to his mother about his uncertainty and receives care in return, treating a close relationship as something worth tending rather than avoiding.

rationales.stimulation: Turning down the promotion is tied to preserving a more open, less constrained life, and he rejects the stagnant path others expect him to want.
rationales.hedonism: He says he wants to "keep my Fridays," explicitly prioritizing enjoyment and free time over career advancement.
rationales.power: He refused the "director thing" despite others thinking he was crazy, turning away from higher status and authority.

rationales.stimulation: He says he "always need[s] something new" and gets bored when life stays the same too long, showing a strong need for change and novelty.
rationales.hedonism: He describes his plan as doing "good work" while also enjoying his life and avoiding becoming someone who eats "sad lunches at a desk," clearly favoring lived enjoyment over career sacrifice.

rationales.stimulation: He says the day's chaos gave him "a rhythm" and "got my blood going," showing that challenge and intensity feel energizing rather than aversive.
rationales.hedonism: The evening centers on sensory pleasure: "windows down, cumbia loud," grilling snapper, smoke, lime, and a quiet perfect dinner with his wife.
rationales.achievement: He solved the shipment crisis, rerouted pallets, and had the corrected order confirmed by four, earning a clear same-day win with an unhappy client.

rationales.stimulation: He signs up for a "weekend mezcal tasting in Oaxaca" and describes himself as "collecting experiences," explicitly seeking novelty.
rationales.hedonism: He sees the trip as something that "looked fun" and booked it immediately, prioritizing anticipated enjoyment.

rationales.stimulation: He spends the weekend trapped in a routine that makes his "brain ... climbing the walls" and says sitting through something familiar felt suffocating, showing a poor fit with repetition and sameness.
rationales.conformity: He sat through the visit "because that's what you do" and bit back what he wanted to say so he would not seem selfish in front of her parents.

rationales.stimulation: He took the day off to surf alone and celebrates the clean waves and the feeling of not becoming the office-bound version of himself, showing a preference for vivid, active experience over routine.
rationales.hedonism: He laughs out loud in the water, eats ceviche at a favorite stand, and describes the trip as proof he is not wasting his life but enjoying it fully.

rationales.security: She had "the whole timeline mapped out" and feels physically distressed when the landlord changes the deposit, showing how strongly she depends on predictability and stable plans.
She reorganizes the kitchen because "when things are out of order I can't think straight" and labels containers to create control in an uncertain living situation.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I'd think about it" when she already knows her answer because she does not want a fight, suppressing her own position instead of asserting it.
rationales.security: Her focus stays on the savings notebook, the delayed apartment deposit, insurance costs, and accident risk, showing strong preference for protection and stability.
rationales.conformity: She withholds what she really thinks so she will not be "the person who always says no," prioritizing smoothness over direct disagreement.

rationales.security: Packing the bag the night before and feeling relief when "the appointment is confirmed" and "nothing can go wrong" shows deliberate effort to create safety and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: She prepares carefully because she wants Sofia to "feel like someone has it handled," centering the child's comfort and care.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I could call someone myself but it's not my house," then waits for Ernesto to decide, deferring her own judgment instead of acting on it.
rationales.security: She hears the heater "rattle," worries what might happen during Sofia's bath, and even boils water "just in case," showing strong concern with preventing danger.
rationales.conformity: Even though she thinks the heater is unsafe, she waits because it is "his parents' house and their water heater," yielding to household expectations and boundaries.

rationales.security: She leans on routine because "my hands know what to do" and reassures herself by checking that "we have everything" twice, using order and preparedness to manage strain.

rationales.conformity: She says she was "afraid of being that older nurse who's always making everything political" and "didn't want to be the difficult one again," showing restraint to avoid social friction.
rationales.universalism: She recognized the comment would deprioritize "the homeless patients, the migrants, the people nobody's advocating for" but "said nothing," failing to defend broader fairness for vulnerable people.

rationales.benevolence: She personally helped by getting "a proper referral to the physiotherapist" and making sure this patient received care instead of being neglected.
rationales.universalism: Her focus is on people the system fails, saying "these people" are constantly let down and she wants to keep them from "fall[ing] through the cracks," which shows concern beyond her immediate circle.

She is preoccupied with "plastic pollution in the North Sea," keeps eco-friendly habits like "I bring my own bags, I cycle," and stays committed despite feeling small, showing sustained environmental concern.

rationales.conformity: She "chose efficiency" and "ticked the box" because the ward was short-staffed, yielding to institutional pressure instead of resisting it.
rationales.universalism: She admits the corners get cut "on the patients who won't complain" and rushed an elderly man with no family advocate, which conflicts with equal care for vulnerable people.

rationales.conformity: When a colleague mocked refugee patients and "everyone laughed," she "didn't say anything," prioritizing non-disruption over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: She stayed silent during a demeaning joke about "the refugee patients 'costing us all money'," which leaves prejudice against an out-group unchallenged.

rationales.conformity: She says the ward manager was "pushing hard" and she "didn't want to hold up the order," so she went along to avoid conflict.
rationales.universalism: She knowingly approved supplies from a company with "terrible labour conditions in Southeast Asia," acting against her stated concern for exploited people beyond her immediate setting.

rationales.conformity: Although she objected verbally, she "abstained because I didn't want to be the only dissenting voice on record," showing deference to group pressure.
rationales.universalism: The motion would reduce access for "undocumented patients," and she admits she protected "my position on the panel" instead of fully opposing harm to a vulnerable group.

rationales.hedonism: She repeatedly chose comfort and convenience, driving because she "didn't want to get wet" and buying from Amazon because it was "cheaper" and fast.
rationales.universalism: She links her choices to environmental harm, describing the diesel car, disposable cup, and Amazon order as a pattern where "the convenience keeps winning," which conflicts with her broader ecological concern.

rationales.conformity: She stayed quiet because she "didn't want to be seen challenging the new director in his first week," showing restraint to fit the room's politics.
rationales.universalism: Fatima's question was about whether non-Dutch-speaking patients would get less time, and by withholding public support she failed to back a fairness concern for a broader group.

rationales.benevolence: She "brought Fatima a coffee" and reassured her directly, offering tangible support to a colleague who had been left hanging.
rationales.universalism: She says she'll "raise her question at the next ethics panel," taking concrete action on an issue tied to equitable treatment in care.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects others' script for stability, saying "People think stability means one thing" and insisting on his own way of structuring work across apps.
rationales.security: He tracks earnings closely, values having "two others" if one app slows down, and sets aside "$200 this week for the car fund," all aimed at protecting stability.
He maintains the car proactively by getting "the oil changed" and keeps the car fund in place because "You never know," emphasizing preparedness and protection against risk.

rationales.self_direction: Instead of freezing, he immediately adapts by "push[ing] harder on Uber and Instacart" and changing his availability to manage the situation himself.
rationales.hedonism: He takes on "early morning grocery runs" that he "hate[s]" and gives up comfort to keep income coming.
rationales.achievement: He responds to the setback with extra effort, reorganizing work across platforms and taking harder jobs to maintain performance.
rationales.power: He says "These companies have all the power" and describes a random complaint leaving him scrambling, showing loss of control over his circumstances.
rationales.security: The deactivation wipes out "roughly 35% of my weekly income," forces him to move money from the car fund, and leaves him hating the uncertainty, all of which undermine stability.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the warehouse path his sister suggests and prefers work where "I'm not depending on someone else deciding I'm worth keeping around," emphasizing autonomy.
rationales.hedonism: He does "twelve hours between Uber and Instacart" even though "my back is killing me," clearly sacrificing comfort.
rationales.achievement: Working a 12-hour day to make "$186" shows sustained effort and focus on keeping earnings up despite the setback.
rationales.security: His reasoning centers on avoiding being "First to get cut when things slow down," choosing the arrangement he believes better protects him from instability.

rationales.self_direction: He focuses on "something I can do" and "something that's in my hands," showing a strong need to act within his own control.
rationales.hedonism: He plans "six-day weeks" and daily monitoring rather than easing up after reinstatement, which goes against present comfort.
rationales.achievement: He sets a concrete recovery plan to rebuild the missing "$300" and commits to extra work until he's "back where I was."
rationales.security: Rebuilding the car fund and checking his rating every day are explicit attempts to anticipate threats and protect his financial footing.
He used a familiar ritual before the client call and said it made him feel "steadier going into the pitch," showing a move toward emotional stability.
rationales.tradition: "Lit the incense" because "my grandmother would approve," tying his action directly to inherited family practice.

rationales.self_direction: He insists that he can "run my own thing," defending an independent path instead of taking over the family business expected of him.

rationales.self_direction: She suppresses the response she actually wanted to give and says nothing back, letting other people's comfort override her own voice.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly stays quiet because speaking sharply "would've made dinner weird," prioritizing social smoothness over expressing herself.

rationales.security: She describes choosing to stay home after calculating daycare costs against salary and organizing the household around a practical, stable arrangement.
rationales.benevolence: Her days are centered on "managing two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule" and wanting to be home with the children in their early years, showing sustained care for close family.

rationales.self_direction: She notices she agrees automatically even when she doesn't need to, describing her own opinion as something she keeps sidelined.
rationales.conformity: She goes along because disagreeing would make her "the difficult one," revealing habitual pressure to avoid social friction.

rationales.self_direction: She says she committed the family to dinner "before I even thought about what I wanted," showing her own preferences getting overruled automatically.
rationales.hedonism: She gives up the quiet family day she wanted, like going to the aquarium or staying home for pizza, and denies herself that simple enjoyment.
rationales.conformity: Her decision is driven by not wanting backing out to "be a thing" or seem selfish when everyone expects them to attend.

rationales.self_direction: She says, "I never say it. I never say it," describing a pattern of not voicing what she wants even when it would have been reasonable to do so.
rationales.conformity: She admits she avoided saying "let me check with Dan first" purely because it would have felt like "making waves," even though no one might have minded.

rationales.stimulation: He rejected the cheaper option solely because the packaging was unfamiliar, showing active avoidance of novelty rather than interest in it.
rationales.security: He spent 'twenty minutes' comparing medication and chose the recognizable brand because unfamiliar packaging felt wrong, prioritizing safety and predictability over savings.
He refuses to risk his financial buffer, saying he built distance from 'the kind of financial stress my parents dealt with' and would 'rather be isolated than exposed.'

rationales.stimulation: He treats the branch move as 'gambling what already works,' showing aversion to uncertainty and challenge rather than attraction to them.
rationales.achievement: He frames a known 'promotion track' as something to delay or avoid, choosing against a clear advancement opportunity.
rationales.security: He favors staying because it preserves what is already stable, explicitly saying the issue is 'not gambling what already works.'
He meal-prepped, replaced expired first aid supplies, and checked flashlight batteries so 'Tomorrow feels manageable because today I made it so,' directly building preparedness and control.
Checking the order 'three times' and reflecting that even a small mistake feels like 'standing on a ledge' shows strong vigilance against things going wrong.
He defends 'holding on tight' and says others 'let go too easily and then act surprised when things fall apart,' showing a strong drive to prevent loss and instability.

rationales.achievement: He takes pride in being the person who keeps a 'team of fourteen people' functioning, emphasizing reliable performance and that 'things don't fall through' because of him.
rationales.security: He explicitly values constant checking and follow-up because otherwise 'things would fall through,' showing active protection against disorder and preventable problems.

rationales.stimulation: A possible restructure sends him into anxious scenario-planning, treating change and uncertainty as threatening rather than energizing.
rationales.security: He has 'six months of expenses saved' and describes having 'protected that buffer carefully,' showing deliberate effort to create financial safety and stability.

rationales.universalism: He values students debating a 'new coastal development project' instead of looking for the 'right' answer, showing engagement with broader environmental and public-interest issues.

rationales.conformity: When the principal told him to 'tone down the activism,' he 'nodded and said I'd think about it,' choosing restrained compliance to maintain institutional harmony.
rationales.universalism: He pushes back on the idea that ocean pollution is a 'foreign problem,' insisting the Arabian Sea matters locally and keeping attention on environmental welfare.

rationales.conformity: He decides to be 'strategic' and teach in ways that 'don't raise red flags,' adapting himself to institutional expectations rather than openly defying them.
rationales.universalism: He keeps focused on students asking questions about 'their own coastline' and wants to remain in place so those issues continue to be taught.

rationales.tradition: Missing one Sunday visit feels like she 'broke something' because the family has gone 'every Sunday since before the kids were born,' showing deep commitment to inherited routine.
rationales.benevolence: Her distress centers on family closeness, especially hearing her father 'putting the plates away' and worrying about letting down the people she loves.

rationales.tradition: She insists on continuing the weekly trip because 'it's just what we do' and refuses to be the one who lets the long-standing family gathering disappear.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps making the difficult visits because otherwise 'My parents eat alone,' prioritizing the wellbeing and connection of close family over convenience.

rationales.tradition: She treasures the potato dish and recipe box as something passed from 'Grand-mère' to 'Maman' to her, and actively hands that inheritance to Léa.
rationales.benevolence: Teaching her daughter to stir the béchamel and linking cooking with how the women in her family 'fed people' shows care expressed through nurturing close others.

rationales.tradition: She values repeated family rituals and stories, ending with 'of course, that's what we do,' and treats passing on the vineyard story as something that must not disappear.
rationales.benevolence: The whole entry centers on gathering family across generations and giving the children meaningful time with relatives, stories, and shared meals.

rationales.tradition: Her thinking is guided by 'a voice in my head that sounds a lot like Maman, and Grand-mère before her' and by wanting to preserve the family model she inherited.
rationales.benevolence: She defines the meaningful work as 'Being here when they come home,' cooking for them, knowing their teachers, and structuring life around her children's daily care.

rationales.hedonism: She gives up sleep and 'the one evening a week I watch something for myself' because preserving the procession matters more than her own comfort or leisure.
rationales.tradition: She volunteers because the Mardi Gras procession is a family and parish custom stretching back to her parents' childhood, and she fears that without intervention 'it just disappears.'
rationales.benevolence: She is willing to be tired to avoid 'explain[ing] to Hugo why the lanterns aren't happening anymore,' showing care for her child and close community ties.

rationales.achievement: She finished "the last piece" after "four months of work" and took pride in the batch looking "exactly the way I wanted," showing clear mastery and completion.
rationales.security: Her relief centered on "the gallery check will clear next week," tying the successful batch directly to financial steadiness.

rationales.self_direction: She defends a life plan that does not need "a company name on it," affirming her own chosen path over relatives' expectations.
rationales.achievement: She frames her work as something she has "built," pointing to pride in what she has accomplished despite lacking conventional status markers.
rationales.security: Listing "the gallery contract through October, the workshop income, the private commissions spreadsheet" shows active effort to secure a stable livelihood.

She spent the morning doing inventory, ordering supplies cautiously because "the exchange rate is worse," and noted that booked workshops "help with the monthly numbers," all focused on maintaining stability.

rationales.self_direction: She values the workshop income because it lets her "say no to design freelance gigs I do not want" and calls it "a trade I chose," emphasizing autonomy.
rationales.security: She describes keeping the workshop income because it is what makes the rest of her choices financially possible, balancing "what I want to make" with "what I need to sell."

rationales.self_direction: She accepted the show because the selected works were "pieces I made for myself" and their artistic meaning mattered more than convenience.
rationales.achievement: Being included in a November group show and valuing that others might "actually connect" with her work reflects pursuit of artistic recognition.
rationales.security: She chose "no artist fee" and a likely "logistics headache," prioritizing the opportunity over financial caution.
Signing a "six-month lease renewal" with the rent held steady gave her a rare fixed point, explicitly reinforcing predictability and stability.

rationales.self_direction: She responded to the accidental glaze result by scraping residue into a jar and deciding, "I will try to figure out what happened," showing independent experimentation.
rationales.achievement: Her frustration came from having pursued the effect through "careful testing, the temperature logs, the glaze ratios," which signals a strong drive toward technical excellence.

rationales.self_direction: She kept pushing her own process by mixing "a new glaze batch" from the residue and adjusting the formula herself.
rationales.achievement: She carefully built a crate for work that had taken "five weeks each" and continued iterative glaze testing, both showing disciplined pursuit of high-quality results.
rationales.security: She wrapped the pieces in "three layers" and built her own crate because she was "not trusting them to someone else's packing job," prioritizing protection and risk reduction.
She tracks "every fils so carefully" so she can truthfully say "everything is fine" and sleep at night, showing strong focus on financial safety.
rationales.benevolence: When her mother sounded worried, she wired her money so "she will not have to ask again for a while," directly supporting a close family member.

rationales.security: She immediately frames the cancellation as "400 dinars I was counting on" and spends an hour staring at the schedule board, showing active concern with preserving financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: She chooses the extra workshop because "it is mine - my studio, my schedule, my terms," explicitly preferring autonomy over better-paid directed work.
rationales.security: She reviews the spreadsheet, weighs two gap-closing options, and picks the one that keeps "the math" working after the cancelled workshop.

rationales.hedonism: He refuses to skip "six hours of good food and good talk," treating immediate enjoyment as worth the cost.
rationales.security: Even while budgeting for insurance and labeling savings "DO NOT touch," he says "I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should," choosing pleasure over financial caution.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects going back to a warehouse because he will not answer to "a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture," clearly prioritizing autonomy.
rationales.security: He dismisses the safer "regular job" option in favor of a less predictable arrangement, putting independence ahead of stability.

rationales.self_direction: Despite doubting whether the trade-off is worth it, he still refuses to "crawl back and prove Graciela right," holding to self-directed independence.
rationales.security: The whole entry centers on rate cuts, canceled jobs, and "the not knowing," with him describing each month as "a guess" and financial stability slipping away.
A "good-sized job" that brings in two days of work makes him feel like he can "breathe again," showing the importance of income stability.
The repair "wiped out everything I'd saved" and left him feeling he has "nothing between me and the next bad surprise," a clear picture of lost safety and stability.

rationales.hedonism: At 1am he sacrifices sleep and comfort to chase two delivery runs, ending the night with "barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas."
rationales.achievement: He pushes himself to take extra jobs in the middle of the night, showing strong effort and willingness to work hard under pressure.
rationales.security: Borrowing a bicycle to squeeze out enough money for "tomorrow's gas" is a direct attempt to keep his finances and work functioning.

rationales.hedonism: He defends the parrilla because it is "the one thing that keeps me sane" and the only time he is not counting pesos or hours, showing clear commitment to present enjoyment and relief.

rationales.security: The painting work comes with a "good rate" and half paid upfront, giving him firmer financial footing.

rationales.self_direction: He says he likes "picking my own hours" and riding around with "nobody telling me where to be," clearly valuing freedom over conventional structure.

rationales.hedonism: He keeps going to the parrilla because it is "the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival," prioritizing enjoyment even under pressure.
rationales.security: He knows "I should be saving what I'm spending here" and worries the good moment "might not last," showing his spending is undercutting financial caution.

rationales.hedonism: He decides to bring "the good Malbec" and looks forward to "eat well with people I like," choosing a small but deliberate pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He finally fixes the chair and patches the wall, then feels good that "the chair is fixed" and the apartment looks better, reflecting satisfaction in competent follow-through.
rationales.security: Repairing the apartment and emphasizing that "right now" things are in order despite uncertain next week shows an effort to restore a sense of stability and control.

rationales.hedonism: He lingers over a pleasant family moment, 'sat on the balcony eating' tamales and noticing the jalapeno shoots, showing active enjoyment of comfort in the present.
rationales.achievement: He emphasizes delivering 'the payment gateway migration three days early' and compares his substantial work to a colleague's praised prototype, showing strong concern with competent performance and recognition.

rationales.self_direction: He suppresses his own judgment even after 'I ran the numbers over the weekend' because 'Diego is the architect,' yielding his independent view to hierarchy.
rationales.security: He chooses to 'document my concerns in the ticket comments so there's a paper trail' and says 'That way I'm covered,' prioritizing protection over confrontation.
rationales.conformity: He stays quiet because speaking up would 'make things awkward' and decides not to 'make it a thing,' showing restraint to preserve workplace smoothness.

rationales.achievement: He inventories concrete accomplishments like 'Seventeen production releases,' mentoring promotions, and cutting CI time, then plans to 'quantify everything' in his review.
rationales.conformity: Even though the self-assessment feels like 'wearing a costume that doesn't fit,' he decides to use 'the strong verbs' the system expects rather than resist the process.

rationales.achievement: He reframes his work in concrete, high-responsibility terms, from reverse-engineering legacy code to mentoring engineers, and completes 'four paragraphs' to represent that impact clearly.
rationales.conformity: He accepts that 'the rules here are different' and that if he doesn't tell Fernando what he has done, 'he genuinely won't know,' so he adapts to the professional norm.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly weighs his current behavior against what was proper 'where I grew up' and recalls how his mother would have viewed self-praise, showing strong reference to inherited cultural norms.
rationales.benevolence: He highlights sustained care for younger colleagues, with 'weekly one-on-ones' and PR reviews that shaped 'how two engineers think,' showing concrete investment in close others' growth.

rationales.hedonism: He marks the good news by taking 'the girls out for helado to celebrate,' deliberately choosing a simple pleasurable moment.
rationales.achievement: He is being put forward for the director role, and the scene centers on his strongest self-assessment and the team's reliability metrics being recognized.
rationales.conformity: He notes they are seeing 'a version of me that learned to write in their language' and accepts that adaptation as part of succeeding in the system.
rationales.benevolence: He holds back a correction because saying it 'would've turned a good moment into a philosophy seminar' and Camila 'deserved to just be happy for me,' prioritizing her experience.

rationales.achievement: He submits a full director packet, including endorsements and a restructuring proposal, showing active pursuit of advancement through visible competence.
rationales.security: He moderates the proposal because 'bold doesn't play well with everyone' on the review panel, carefully minimizing downside risk.
rationales.conformity: He frames criticism as 'building on existing strengths' instead of naming problems directly, tailoring his message to institutional expectations.

rationales.achievement: The entry is centered on a high-stakes panel interview where he delivers prepared answers to move forward professionally.
rationales.conformity: He says 'I said what I prepared. Didn't improvise, didn't overshoot,' showing deliberate restraint and adherence to expected interview behavior.

rationales.power: She explicitly draws the line with Andres, saying 'one more time and I'm finding someone else' and 'The gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set,' asserting authority over her space.
rationales.security: She acts because a late installer made her cover in front of a collector and she worries that 'that's exactly why I can't let things slide,' protecting business stability.
rationales.conformity: She enforces punctual, orderly behavior because she 'can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table,' upholding clear professional norms.

rationales.achievement: She describes the finished triptych with pride, saying 'It works' and that rare moment when what she made matched what she envisioned, signaling successful execution of demanding work.
rationales.security: She emphasizes paying rent on time, having 'the next three months covered since January,' and updating her mother on the commission check, showing active focus on financial steadiness.

rationales.power: She lets the investor keep 'talking over me' and says she 'smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back,' which shows a failure to assert control in an important negotiation.

rationales.self_direction: She values having a gallery where 'every corner of that space reflects a decision I made,' emphasizing independence in how the space is run.
rationales.power: She studies 'who commands a space' and says that when she had to negotiate every wall placement, nothing felt fully hers, showing strong concern with authority and control over the environment.

rationales.hedonism: She deliberately savors a 'Lazy Sunday,' sleeping late, making arepas, and sitting on the balcony enjoying the street sounds.
rationales.security: Even on a day off she reviews invoices because she likes 'knowing exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out,' showing active concern for predictability and reserves.
rationales.tradition: She connects her money-tracking instinct to family inheritance, recalling her father keeping 'a paper ledger for his tire shop,' which ties present choices to familial practice.

rationales.achievement: She successfully finalizes an exhibition lineup, takes a calculated risk on a new artist, and grounds it in collector commitments and strong March projections.
rationales.power: She says curating means 'I'm deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't' and that 'I've earned that,' showing comfort with gatekeeping influence.
rationales.security: She notes 'the reserve fund is untouched, insurance renewal is handled' and that when finances are solid she can think clearly, highlighting deliberate protection of stability.

rationales.self_direction: She immediately pushes back with 'we need to clarify terms before anything moves forward,' refusing to let someone else define her arrangement for her.
rationales.power: Her reaction centers on title and authority, saying someone thinks they can 'slide into a title I haven't offered' and that 'the boundary is drawn,' which is a direct assertion of control.

rationales.achievement: The show preparation is executed cleanly, with artists delivering on time and the opening set exactly as planned.
rationales.power: She takes satisfaction that 'everything is exactly where I placed it' and that invitations went to 'the right people,' emphasizing control over both space and access.

rationales.achievement: She measures success through concrete wins like 'Sold four pieces in the first two hours' and a new artist sale on opening night.
rationales.power: A collector saying he'd 'been hearing my name' matters deeply to her, and she frames the moment as building a reputation in the city's art scene.
rationales.security: She stresses that 'the reserve is intact' and wants her mother to know the gallery is no longer 'some reckless bet,' showing concern with durable financial footing.

rationales.self_direction: She defends her independent path, noting she 'left a stable partnership to build this alone' and trusts her own judgment over a stranger's criticism.
rationales.power: She responds internally with 'My gallery, my choices, my track record,' centering ownership and control over the direction of the space.
rationales.security: She explicitly justifies her choices by 'keeping my lights on and my artists paid,' prioritizing viability and dependable operations.

rationales.power: She says of artist selection, 'I won't give that up,' making clear that decision authority is the core boundary in the negotiation.
rationales.security: Her hesitation is tied to funding risk, since 'I also can't fund the expansion alone and the bank terms are worse,' showing strong concern with practical stability.

rationales.hedonism: He skips his own needs, noting he "hadn't eaten since a cheese sandwich at eleven" and that even the plants "aren't getting my best," which shows present comfort being sacrificed.
rationales.benevolence: He repeatedly cares for close family by picking up Lotte's children, buying them treats, and making Lotte tea and doing washing when she looked unwell.
He gives up his Sunday morning to fix Lotte's leak, sits with her while she cries, and stays nearby with the kids, showing sustained care for close family.
He spends the afternoon with Lotte's children and keeps carrying the emotional burden himself, saying "I carry it" rather than confirming her fear that she is ruining his weekends.
Even while tired from work, he is thinking about whether Lotte will need him and decides he needs to "show up back" for Jeroen too, reflecting active concern for close relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the future as something he may not be freely choosing, asking whether it is "something I'm choosing or something that's choosing me" and saying putting the wrench down "doesn't feel like an option."
rationales.benevolence: He continues to anchor Lotte's support despite the growing weight, saying if he steps back "the gap isn't filled by someone else," which shows strong commitment to close family care.

rationales.hedonism: He fully embraces enjoyment, describing that he "drank too many Jupilers, talked rubbish, laughed until my stomach hurt" and cycled home feeling lighter.
rationales.benevolence: Going to Jeroen's birthday and valuing that "he's glad I came" shows intentional investment in a close friendship.

rationales.hedonism: He explicitly rejects centering his own wants, saying thinking about what he wants "doesn't change what Lotte needs," and describes a drained state where "everything costs a bit more than it should."
rationales.benevolence: He defends continuing to help his sister and her children, insisting "I'd rather be tired than be the kind of person who steps away when things get hard."

rationales.conformity: He restrains himself and follows the expected path in the moment, admitting "I wanted to say something" but instead went to his desk and kept doing his assigned work.
rationales.universalism: Seeing "a child no older than ten carrying bricks" triggers broad moral concern about exploitation, and he is troubled by the gap between his job and "what I believe matters."

rationales.self_direction: He independently re-evaluates his assumptions, concluding he has "forgotten what survival looks like" instead of staying with his initial reaction.
rationales.tradition: His mother's reminder about carrying water as a child reconnects him to where he came from, and he treats that past as morally grounding rather than dismissing it.
rationales.benevolence: Calling his mother and seriously taking in her perspective shows attentiveness to a close family bond.
rationales.universalism: He reflects on his privilege in relation to the child's labor and broadens the issue beyond himself, showing concern for people outside his immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses a new path of action on his own by enrolling in a course aimed at "building accessible software for low-resource settings."
rationales.stimulation: Enrolling in a new online course shows an active move toward fresh learning and challenge.
rationales.achievement: He takes concrete, goal-directed steps by both sending the transfer and signing up for training, not just reflecting abstractly.
rationales.tradition: Supporting the village school ties his actions back to his place of origin rather than distancing himself from it.
rationales.benevolence: Sending money to the village school is a concrete act of support for people he feels close responsibility toward.
rationales.universalism: Both the school transfer and accessible-software course are oriented toward improving conditions for underserved people beyond his household.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the default career script, realizing "the question itself reveals different assumptions about what work is for" and exploring a personally chosen purpose.
rationales.stimulation: He is energized by unfamiliar learning, stating "The course is fascinating" as he engages with new design constraints and ideas.
rationales.security: He openly considers choices that may destabilize his finances, acknowledging that his daughter's tuition comes from corporate work and that "the math doesn't always work out cleanly."
rationales.conformity: He pushes against the prevailing norm voiced by Raghav that skills should mainly increase "market value," rejecting that narrow expectation.
rationales.tradition: Remembering his grandmother's pattern recognition in kolam designs shows respect for inherited cultural knowledge as something intellectually valuable.
rationales.universalism: He wants his expertise to "serve people who actually need it," showing concern for broader human welfare over corporate gain.

rationales.self_direction: He does not simply accept Lakshmi's suggestion; he examines his own motives and admits an honest, self-authored uncertainty about what being "useful" would mean.
rationales.power: He undercuts status-based motivation by questioning whether corporate proof like "pull requests merged" and "performance reviews completed" is a meaningful basis for worth.
rationales.security: His hesitation about leaving paid work is driven by fear and the need for dependable footing, showing strong orientation toward stability and protection from loss.
rationales.universalism: Even while uncertain, his concrete example of usefulness is "helping someone learn to code who couldn't afford classes," which centers people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.conformity: He spends the meeting "nodding along, contributing where required," showing restraint and compliance with workplace expectations despite inner disagreement.
rationales.universalism: He keeps comparing wasteful corporate effort to "how many village school teachers' salaries could be paid," revealing persistent concern for broader fairness and human need.

rationales.self_direction: He begins reorienting himself around a self-chosen purpose, asking what it would look like to "un-bury" the reasons he became an engineer.
rationales.tradition: His reflection returns to formative beliefs and family teaching, especially his role as a father who taught that "every small action matters."
rationales.benevolence: The conversation with Priya is emotionally significant and reciprocal, showing investment in a close family relationship rather than detachment.
rationales.universalism: He reconnects with the goal of "build[ing] things that would make life better," expressing concern for people beyond his own circle.

rationales.self_direction: He proactively asks HR to reduce to four days a week so he can redirect time toward teaching, prioritizing a self-chosen path over the standard full-time track.
rationales.stimulation: Changing his work arrangement and starting community teaching introduces challenge and novelty rather than staying in a familiar routine.
rationales.achievement: He takes concrete, measurable action by negotiating reduced hours and scheduling the "First session" rather than just imagining a change.
rationales.power: He accepts less status and compensation, explicitly choosing adjusted pay over maximizing professional standing.
rationales.security: He knowingly gives up financial certainty, admitting "The money difference isn't trivial" even though they can manage.
rationales.conformity: Reducing corporate work to teach at a community center pushes against the expected career script instead of simply following it.
rationales.benevolence: Teaching domestic workers and their children is a hands-on effort to help specific people directly.
rationales.universalism: The plan is explicitly aimed at a community center serving marginalized workers and children, reflecting concern beyond his immediate relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He abandons his prepared lesson plan and adapts to what the students actually need, then commits to "showing up again next week" and "starting where they are."
rationales.stimulation: Teaching in an unfamiliar context forces him into a new, challenging situation that departs sharply from his usual expertise.
rationales.power: He gives up the superior expert stance, admitting he had "parachuted in with my fancy knowledge" and choosing humility over authority.
rationales.benevolence: He stays patient with Kamala and the class, spending the last hour on basic skills instead of centering his own plan.
rationales.universalism: His effort is directed toward expanding access for people who have been excluded from digital literacy, such as a woman learning to type her name for the first time.

rationales.self_direction: He reconnects to a personally chosen purpose, saying he "remembered why I wanted to do this in the first place" rather than acting from obligation alone.
rationales.hedonism: He allows himself to feel joy in the moment, lingering on Kamala's smile and calling it "this one victory" that felt real.
rationales.achievement: He recognizes a concrete success in helping create a result, noting "Something I helped create" when Kamala typed her name.
rationales.benevolence: His satisfaction comes from a direct, personal impact on someone he is teaching, not from abstract metrics.
rationales.universalism: He frames the moment as evidence that "Every small action matters," tying the teaching work to broader concern for people beyond his family.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to say "I wasn't finished" but instead "just sat there," showing he suppressed his own voice rather than authoring the situation.
rationales.power: He describes being interrupted, ignored, and watching Henrik get credit for "what I'd already done better," which reflects a clear loss of influence and standing.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because pushing back might make him look "petty or desperate," explicitly restraining himself to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.achievement: He takes pride in showing parents "exactly where their kid improved" and wants to be seen as the teacher who is highly organized and effective.
rationales.power: "When they trust you, you can actually change things" and "I put myself in a position to" frame competence as a route to influence over outcomes.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agreed to mentor a student through the IB application and focused on changes that made students "more engaged," showing active support for those in his care.

rationales.self_direction: He is forced to "sit here and wait" for others to approve work he has already completed, underscoring blocked autonomy over his own project.
rationales.power: "If they'd just give me direct access to the budget approval process I could have this sorted in a day" shows frustration at having no control over decisions affecting his work.

rationales.achievement: He was chosen because his "documentation is the most thorough" and is already preparing so he can have "the most polished submission in the room."
rationales.power: He wants district coordinators to "know my name" so he becomes "someone people actually listen to," making influence and status a clear aim.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I caved because I could feel the room turning," showing he abandoned his own judgment instead of holding his line.
rationales.power: The meeting turns against his plan and he yields to Karin's pushback, leaving him without influence over the decision.
rationales.conformity: He changed course because he "could feel the room turning," which is direct evidence of bending to group pressure to maintain acceptance.

rationales.achievement: He defends the systems he "built" for his classes and is especially concerned that criticism of his lesson pacing could damage others' view of his competence.
rationales.power: He pulled the assistant aside to explain "how my classroom runs" and is focused on correcting Elsa's impression before it spreads, emphasizing control over his domain and reputation.

rationales.achievement: He spent the weekend refining the presentation, was told it was "exactly the level of detail the panel looks for," and takes pride that "nobody else bothered to put the work in. I did."
rationales.power: Being put forward for a cross-school working group and gaining recognition for successful proposals both point to growing influence beyond his classroom.
rationales.benevolence: He organized the Year 9 field trip and notes students are excited because it is "the first time a teacher has organised something like this for their year group," showing effort on their behalf.

rationales.power: "I set the rhythm of these meetings" and his quick redirection of Camila both show active control over the group's direction.
rationales.conformity: He prioritizes keeping everyone "on time, on topic" and dismisses Camila's idea because "we had an agenda," emphasizing rule-following and restraint to preserve order.

rationales.power: "The department works because someone holds the line. That someone is me" makes authority and control over the group the centerpiece of the entry.
rationales.conformity: He justifies shutting Camila down because otherwise they would never "get through anything" and says she "will learn how things work here," stressing adherence to established norms.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to claim "those were my numbers" but instead "smiled" and "nodded," suppressing his own position rather than asserting it.
rationales.power: His analysis is repurposed by Ríos while he sits there "like I am attending someone else's meeting," showing a clear loss of influence and ownership.
rationales.conformity: He stays publicly agreeable because pushing back "would have looked petty," explicitly subordinating his reaction to social expectations.

rationales.self_direction: "You do not wait for permission, you present the solution" shows deliberate independent action instead of waiting for approval.
rationales.achievement: He proactively spotted exam conflicts, fixed them himself, and sent a marked-up solution, demonstrating competence and a results-oriented standard.
rationales.power: Sending corrections "marked in red" and cc'ing relevant people reflects an assertive attempt to shape decisions and direct the process.
rationales.benevolence: He says he intervened because the draft had conflicts that would affect "my students," showing concrete effort to protect people in his immediate care.

rationales.achievement: He chose to eat lunch at his desk grading quizzes because he "get[s] more done alone," prioritizing productivity and task completion over socializing.

rationales.hedonism: He rejects Patricia's suggestion to relax, insisting "when I relax, things slip," which treats pleasure and ease as something to resist rather than value.
rationales.achievement: He values being thanked for work that "saved them a headache" and says "you do good work, someone notices," tying effort to recognized competence.
rationales.power: "The man who controls the calendar controls the operation" and his habit of knowing what is happening before others show a strong drive to maintain control.
rationales.security: He constantly reviews schedules because that is "how you keep things from falling apart" and fears that letting go would lead to chaos, reflecting a strong priority on stability and predictability.

rationales.power: "She married a man who takes charge of things" and "that is who I am at school and at the junta and at home" make command and authority central to his self-concept.

He is stuck waiting for approval from someone else and reads the silence as a sign that she does not "respect the department heads enough to respond promptly," highlighting blocked status and influence.

rationales.self_direction: He wanted to push back on sharing the proposal but instead said "sounds good" despite believing it "does not sound good," suppressing his own judgment.
rationales.power: His idea is being turned into a joint project where his expertise gets "diluted into consensus," showing a loss of control and ownership.
rationales.conformity: He goes along because "you cannot argue against that without looking difficult," explicitly yielding to social pressure to appear cooperative.

rationales.self_direction: He says he "could have pushed" but instead "just nodded," showing he suppressed his own judgment rather than acting on it.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because he was "worried about being the guy who derails standup" and because he was taught to "keep your head down," showing restraint to avoid social friction.
rationales.universalism: He centers harm to migrant workers, noting the rejected transactions affect "the kind migrant workers use to send money home" and that "the people on the other end... aren't abstractions."
He spent his morning helping an injured worker "draft a letter to MOM" and stays focused on the man's unjust situation rather than his own credit.
He says "we can afford a car, we just choose not to" and reflects on not feeling the pull to keep "upgrading," suggesting a deliberate lower-consumption choice over status consumption.
He "scrolled past an article about plastic waste in the Java Sea" and "kept eating from the styrofoam container," choosing convenience while ignoring environmental harm.

rationales.self_direction: He "didn't say anything" and admits he stayed silent because he was "scared of being seen differently," showing he yielded his own convictions to others' perceptions.
rationales.conformity: At the dinner table he "just sat there because it was a team dinner and I didn't want to make it awkward," prioritizing smooth social relations over speaking up.
rationales.universalism: He recognizes the remark targets people treated as "somehow less" and connects it to what workers face, yet chooses silence in the face of prejudice.

rationales.benevolence: He describes cooking dinner "the three of us" and feeling reassured with Mei Ling and Xiao Yu, emphasizing care and connection within his immediate family.
rationales.universalism: The outing centers beach cleanup, with Xiao Yu picking up "bits of plastic from the sand," and he explicitly says "the habits matter. The noticing matters."
He spent the morning helping "two new guys file their claims" at the worker center and frames it as a meaningful "second life" of service beyond his own circle.

rationales.self_direction: He says "I could have pushed back" but instead "said okay," acting against his own judgment about what mattered.
rationales.conformity: He complied when "the PM asked me to cut corners" and chose the path that fit the launch pressure rather than resisting it.
rationales.universalism: He knows the shortcut affects "real people's money" and that "the ones who suffer are the ones who can least afford it," yet he still chose what was "easy for me."

rationales.self_direction: He acknowledges "I'll escalate next time" but again only "nodded," showing continued failure to act on his own convictions.
rationales.conformity: Even after his lead says it "can't squeeze it in," he goes along with the planned priorities instead of challenging them.
rationales.universalism: He sees that people in Bangladesh and Myanmar "can't send money to their families" because of the issue, yet still lets the delay stand.
He admits he has been "choosing convenience over doing what I know matters," referring to a problem that harms people trying to send money home.

rationales.security: He says yes to covering the copay "right away" and only then looks at his account asking "where is this money supposed to come from," showing a choice that strains his own stability.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately agrees to help when "Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay," putting a close family member's need first.

rationales.hedonism: He has not taken "more than a day off in probably six months" and even imagines a day off turning into calculations about lost income instead of rest.
rationales.security: He works back-to-back long shifts because the "holiday surge is good for earnings" and even on a hypothetical day off worries about "how much money I'm losing."
rationales.benevolence: He used his earnings to "drop off the pharmacy money to Mom" and refused her offer to return some, telling her to "keep it in case something else comes up."

rationales.security: He weighs Christmas primarily through financial necessity, noting those days are "usually the best earning days" and that one day of driving could cover "half my car insurance payment."

rationales.self_direction: She contrasts "pretending to care about supply chain management" in a lecture hall with actually building the business in real life, showing a deliberate move toward her own path.
rationales.stimulation: She says she "just wanted to touch everything" at the warehouse and is energized by the unfamiliar, hands-on sourcing experience.

rationales.hedonism: After the pop-up she celebrates by spending "way too much on cocktails" because "the music was perfect" and it "felt exactly right."
rationales.achievement: She highlights that the pop-up "went well," they "sold eleven pieces," and she enjoys strangers liking "something I made with my hands."

rationales.self_direction: She resists moving in together because she wants "space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining."
rationales.security: She pulls back from cohabitation because it would become "this whole plan" with routines and shared obligations, rejecting a more stable arrangement.

rationales.achievement: She pushed through until "the last sample" matched "exactly how I pictured it" and ends the night with the satisfaction of a completed standard met.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother "I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan," asserting her own chosen path despite family pressure.
rationales.conformity: She refuses to play the expected role of the cautious daughter at the family gathering, saying she is tired of being "the cautionary tale" and rejecting the backup-plan script.

rationales.self_direction: She values having "space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining," emphasizing personal autonomy.
rationales.stimulation: She describes shared domestic routine as intolerable because "there's no surprise" and she wants the freedom for spontaneous choices instead.
rationales.security: The possibility of signing a lease feels threatening because it would lock in "routines and splitting rent and groceries," and she prefers to keep things less settled.
Diya pausing the brand makes it feel like "the floor shifted," and she is left "not sure what I'm supposed to do with half a brand," showing acute destabilization.

rationales.power: He is angry that Kramer presented the results "like they were his," showing strong concern with recognition, influence, and whose name gets attached to success.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because "picking a fight on camera would only hurt me," deliberately restraining himself to avoid conflict and reputational fallout.

rationales.hedonism: He says Lotte opened a special bottle and he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because he was fixated on the slight, putting pleasure aside.
rationales.achievement: He frames six years of building the region and wanting his name attached to the outcome as proof of competence and career progress.
rationales.power: He explicitly links public credit to advancement: "It's how you move up" and how people think of him for "the next VP seat."

rationales.hedonism: He canceled dinner at "that new place in Katendrecht" because work left him too exhausted to enjoy it.
rationales.achievement: When two leads called in sick, he spent "six hours on the floor sorting parcels," prioritizing operational performance despite the physical cost.

rationales.hedonism: Even on a "Saturday sailing trip" he was checking emails and says he "barely noticed the water," showing work crowding out enjoyment.
rationales.power: His frustration centers on being sidelined by Kramer and on what it means to be the person who "run[s] things," reflecting an ongoing focus on status and control.
rationales.conformity: He says Kramer was condescending but he "smiled through anyway" and raised the issue in a "measured" way, carefully managing himself to avoid open friction.

rationales.achievement: Securing approval to hire for the Antwerp hub reflects concrete progress on a major work objective and expansion responsibility.
rationales.power: He celebrates that it is "my budget, my picks, no committee" and says it feels like "actual authority," directly emphasizing control over decisions.

rationales.hedonism: He calls the evening with "good food, the right people, candles on the canal" and Lotte in her element "the kind of evening I live for," clearly embracing sensory pleasure.
rationales.power: He notes the guests' industry influence and says he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth," treating the dinner as a way to build status and leverage.

rationales.hedonism: He gave up a Bruges weekend with a "rooftop terrace" and "Michelin-star restaurant" to work, then realized he could have been there instead of "eating cheese toast."
rationales.achievement: He keeps pushing the permit problem because he "can't afford" delay with the VP shortlist coming, putting career progress ahead of rest.
rationales.benevolence: He chose work over time with "the person I like most in the world," despite seeing that the extra Saturday effort changed little and left Lotte going without him.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately took Friday off, put his "phone in a drawer," then sailed, grilled fish, drank Riesling, and watched the light change on the water.
rationales.power: He overrode the site manager's layout himself because his plan would create a bottleneck, showing a strong impulse to take control and impose his judgment.

rationales.hedonism: The possible move would mean "more time in transit" and "fewer evenings on the terrace," with enjoyment and domestic pleasures treated as likely sacrifices.
rationales.power: He is pulled by "more status, more scope, more say in how the company runs" and describes the role as what he has been "manoeuvring towards for years."

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects the idea that benefits and a standard path are the only real options.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts waiting for "some dude to get his Thai food" with rappelling into a canyon fire and thinking "this is what life should feel like," suggesting his current routine falls short of the intensity he craves.
rationales.achievement: He tracks concrete progress, citing "$800 in sales this month" for the drone business and calling it "momentum."
rationales.security: He explicitly resists "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring," dismissing stability as the goal.

rationales.self_direction: He insists he is not taking "some cubicle job" to satisfy other people and defends building a path others do not recognize.
rationales.achievement: He lays out a concrete benchmark: if the drone business hits "$2000/month by December," that covers rent and creates runway toward full-time work.
rationales.security: He rejects Kevin's stability-focused argument about a 401k and conventional long-term safety, favoring a riskier self-built path.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back against social pressure from Rachel's brother and refuses to reshape his life just so others can stop worrying about him.

rationales.self_direction: He reaches out to Dario and resonates with "stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong," reaffirming his own unconventional path.
rationales.conformity: The whole entry resists others' approval standards, especially the pressure to justify himself to people who have already judged him.

rationales.self_direction: He says being asked for "stability" feels like being asked to "become someone else," strongly defending the right to live on his own terms.
rationales.achievement: He keeps framing the drone work as "building toward something" and would "rather be broke and building something" than settle for comfort.
rationales.security: He openly rejects the stable path Rachel seems to want, saying his parents had "stable jobs and they were miserable" and preferring uncertainty.
rationales.benevolence: Rachel asks "Where am I in it?" and he admits he has been focused on proving "my way works" without thinking enough about whether she wanted to be part of that ride.

rationales.stimulation: He says driving is "just time traded for money" and that the drone work "doesn't hit the same," describing a life that feels flat rather than energizing.
rationales.achievement: He no longer feels his current work matters and says he is "not sure" the bridge leads anywhere, signaling a loss of confidence in effective progress.

rationales.conformity: He swallows his pride and says yes to counseling, recognizing that the old instinct to treat help as weakness is something he needs to restrain.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts that Rachel "deserves to know the stakes" and that if he succeeds or fails, "she's there," showing real concern for the relationship rather than only his own project.

rationales.stimulation: He describes himself as "wired to move" and fears stillness so strongly that it feels like "dying," showing a deep drive toward motion and intensity.
rationales.achievement: He says happiness has usually been tied to being "good at it," doing something "hard," or proving "the concept worked," linking fulfillment to striving and accomplishment.
rationales.security: He is unsettled by the idea of not working, by injury, and by his father's collapse after retirement; stillness and stability feel threatening rather than comforting.
rationales.benevolence: He keeps returning to what this means for Rachel and says he wants "the right kind of motion" to be "something that includes her," showing care for preserving the relationship.

rationales.power: He keeps replaying being talked over and notes that "if it were Thomas or Sven nobody would have talked over them," showing strong concern with status and whose voice carries weight.
rationales.conformity: He insists meetings should have "a basic level of order" and that "the chair should have handled it," emphasizing proper roles and norm-following.

rationales.power: He contrasts a meaningless "thumbs-up on Teams" with someone seeking him out because "someone values your input enough to come find you," which centers influence and standing.
He is upset that he has "no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines" and that "someone else holds the pen," showing a clear desire for control over outcomes.

rationales.hedonism: After the meeting he "went home and just sat on the couch for an hour" and "didn't even eat properly," showing the experience actively undercut present comfort and enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: He "built out the entire rollback scenario," "spent the weekend on the risk matrix," and is stung that thorough work was "waved away," highlighting excellence and desire for competent work to matter.
rationales.power: What hurts most is being overruled by the louder voice in the room, with others defaulting to Thomas and treating his analysis like "a suggestion," showing sensitivity to influence and rank dynamics.

rationales.achievement: He frames the omission in terms of being on "the senior management pipeline" and not wanting to wake up "three years from now still in the same chair," showing strong concern with advancement and recognized merit.
rationales.power: He compares himself to peers who were nominated and recalls pushing for a prior title change with a one-pager, showing active attention to status, promotion, and how decision-makers see him.

rationales.achievement: He is bothered that he "identified the problem" and set the direction but the visible output and praise went elsewhere, showing concern that his contribution be recognized.
rationales.power: The line "I set the direction, I identified the problem, I allocated the resource" shows he is focused on having his managerial influence acknowledged rather than disappearing behind subordinates.
rationales.conformity: He stays silent because correcting Clara in public would look "petty" and "nobody wants to be that manager," showing restraint to preserve smooth group norms.

rationales.benevolence: He spent the one-on-one helping Daan map trade-offs and says the part he actually likes is "someone coming to me with a problem, me helping them think it through," showing genuine investment in supporting someone close in his work circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says scrubbing off the indigo felt like "erasing something" and did it despite feeling she "shouldn't have had to," showing suppression of something personally important to her.
rationales.conformity: She removed the stains because it was "easier than sitting through another silent dinner" and says she "pick[s her] fights," showing behavior shaped by avoiding disapproval and keeping peace.

rationales.self_direction: She wanted to say "my son likes drawing, why is that a problem?" but instead smiled and agreed to encourage something else, showing she did not act on her own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Her explanation, "You just don't, do you? Not in that setting," shows she deferred to expected behavior in a formal social context rather than challenging it.

rationales.tradition: She makes breakfast "the way Appa used to" and is transported back to her childhood home, showing active connection to inherited family practice.

rationales.self_direction: Later she works on "a new indigo resist pattern" and savors "two hours of silence" with "my hands doing what they wanted to do," showing autonomous creative choice.
rationales.tradition: She helps set up the community puja and makes rangoli "the way my grandmother taught me," explicitly preserving and passing on inherited practice.
She agrees to teach Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama because "it's important" and notes that Appa made sure she knew it by heart, showing commitment to transmitting family-religious continuity.
She frames the lesson as "this thread that connects us backward through generations," explicitly valuing continuity with inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Vihaan to feel "the warmth of sitting together with something that matters," focusing on nurturing her child rather than merely enforcing rote performance.

rationales.self_direction: She independently built a fermentation vat from an old manual, tracks her own process, and is seriously considering whether to take the order, showing self-directed pursuit of her craft.
rationales.achievement: She catalogues fourteen shades, labels each by mordant, dip count, and fabric type, and evaluates production limits for a paid order, showing disciplined competence and goal-oriented work.
rationales.benevolence: When Vihaan asks if he can dip his hand in the vat, she lets him join in, making space for a close family member in something important to her.

rationales.self_direction: She clearly claims that the afternoon hours are "mine" and wants to pursue home and enterprise together rather than accept a single prescribed role.
rationales.conformity: Her statement that she needs people to stop treating her available hours as "leftover time that belongs to the house" pushes against expected domestic norms rather than accommodating them.

rationales.self_direction: She feels her project is being diminished and ends the evening by saying nothing, finishing dinner, clearing plates, and retreating upstairs, showing her own aims being suppressed in the moment.
rationales.conformity: Even after being put on the spot, she does not challenge the framing at the table and slips back into expected household behavior by quietly "clear[ing] the plates."

rationales.self_direction: She says she wants the same quiet certainty Appa had: "of course I do this, it's mine," which is a direct claim to owning her path without needing permission.
rationales.conformity: Her frustration that everyone only asked whether it would "interfere with anything" and her desire to be treated as naturally entitled to the work reject the household role expectations being imposed on her.

rationales.tradition: She takes the boys to the Ganesh temple for the monthly abhishekam, and the priest remarks on "keeping these things alive," showing active maintenance of inherited religious practice.
rationales.benevolence: Her pride that Vihaan recited the verses unprompted and her effort to carry this forward with her children show care for the growth and belonging of close family.

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the order and concludes, "I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough," explicitly prioritizing her own chosen path.
rationales.stimulation: Saying yes feels "terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water," showing she embraces a challenging, uncertain step rather than staying with the familiar.
rationales.achievement: She commits to a concrete February order with specific deliverables, turning her craft into a real project with scale and execution demands.
rationales.benevolence: She welcomes Ravi's help and says yes when Vihaan asks to help "with the blue," making the decision in a way that includes close family rather than shutting them out.

rationales.power: He was "talked right over" and "just sat there," describing the moment as a loss of standing and control rather than an assertion of authority.
rationales.conformity: Instead of challenging Pieter in the meeting, he restrained himself and let the interruption stand, prioritizing immediate social smoothness over confrontation.

rationales.achievement: He ran the review with "just my numbers, my narrative," taking ownership of performance and competence in a high-visibility setting.
rationales.power: "Felt like the division is finally mine" shows satisfaction in influence, control, and being the central authority in the room.
He fixates on having to wait "like some junior waiting for permission," framing the situation as an intolerable loss of control over his division's work.
rationales.conformity: He "thought better of" going to the CEO and accepted the cross-department bottleneck instead of breaking rank or escalating outside the usual chain.
