rationales.hedonism: Even with wine and music he keeps doing the math on unfinished tasks and ends up working two more hours, sacrificing enjoyment instead of settling into it.
rationales.achievement: He cannot stop measuring whether he "finish[ed] enough" and returns to work for relief, showing strong orientation toward productivity and performance.
rationales.benevolence: He explains that if he is not working, "someone on my team might be stuck," showing attention to supporting close coworkers.

rationales.hedonism: The day centers on ordinary enjoyment and bodily wellbeing through a run, a shower, a stir fry, and a restful night.
rationales.security: He describes a steady, orderly routine with chores, groceries, exercise, and bed at "a reasonable hour," emphasizing maintenance and stability.
rationales.benevolence: He makes time to call his mom and stays engaged in the family conversation, reflecting ongoing care for close relationships.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day, he skips the bath he wanted and says he could not even argue that he "deserved an hour off," clearly denying himself rest and comfort.
rationales.achievement: He opens the laptop to prep for tomorrow's review even when exhausted, prioritizing performance obligations over recovery.

rationales.hedonism: The weekend includes sleeping in, cooking, beers with a friend, football, and going to bed early, showing straightforward enjoyment and rest.
rationales.benevolence: Meeting Chris for beers and spending the afternoon together reflects investment in a close friendship.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to leave the office rhythm behind and sit at the coffee shop without a laptop, actively making space for a slower version of the day.
rationales.hedonism: He enjoys the cortado, the light, and the simple act of watching people, describing it as "genuinely nice" and rediscovering a small pleasure.
rationales.security: His hesitation comes from not believing that what he leaves undone "isn't going to fall apart," showing how strongly he is oriented toward keeping things under control and safe.

rationales.hedonism: He goes to a restaurant he wanted to try, but spends "half the meal" thinking about the review deck and leaves feeling he had not really been there, undermining enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: Work obligations intrude so strongly that an unfinished quarterly review deck dominates his attention even during dinner.
rationales.benevolence: He still shows up for dinner with a friend despite the pressure, indicating effort to maintain a close relationship.

rationales.security: She takes satisfaction in the fact that "Nobody left upset" and there will be "No difficult calls tomorrow," reflecting a strong preference for avoiding disruption and preserving stability.
rationales.conformity: She carefully says "all the right things" and prefers that to blunt truth because she would rather be tired than "cause that kind of mess," showing restraint to maintain harmony and meet expectations.
rationales.benevolence: Her careful handling of twenty-three families is aimed at protecting others' feelings and preventing conflict, which reflects attentive care toward people in her immediate school community.

rationales.self_direction: She says she did what was "expected of me" and worries that being that person may mean giving up "other things I want to be," showing suppression of her own preferred identity.
rationales.security: She follows protocol "exactly" because looking away would imply rules are flexible, reflecting a strong drive to preserve order and institutional reliability.
rationales.conformity: She documents the cheating, submits the report, and acts "Everything by the book," clearly prioritizing rules and expected conduct over personal discomfort.
rationales.tradition: She explicitly leans on her mother's example that "A teacher who ignores cheating isn't a teacher," showing deference to an inherited model of proper conduct.
rationales.benevolence: The loss that hurts is Jia-Hui's trust and no longer being seen as the teacher who helped after school, showing how much she values caring relationships with her students.

rationales.achievement: She centers her response on being "focused on work right now" and how exhausting exam period is, showing priority on meeting her professional obligations.
rationales.conformity: She handles her mother's recurring matchmaking pressure with the familiar "same as last time" answer, maintaining social smoothness instead of confronting the expectation.
rationales.benevolence: She interprets her mother's behavior as meaning well and responds gently rather than dismissively, indicating care for a close family relationship.

rationales.self_direction: She says she has "started to forget what I want when no one's watching" because most of her life is driven by what others expect, showing her own preferences being crowded out.
rationales.hedonism: She gives up a ceramics class she wants and settles for YouTube videos instead, denying herself a simple pleasure and personally meaningful pastime.
rationales.conformity: She is the "voice of the department," smiles politely at comments, and avoids signing up for ceramics because of her parents' likely disappointment, showing strong deference to expectations.
rationales.tradition: Her explanation that "That's not how my family works. We show up for each other" ties her choice directly to maintaining established family patterns.
rationales.benevolence: She forgoes the class partly to preserve her parents' usual visiting time and avoid upsetting them, prioritizing close family over her own desire.

rationales.self_direction: She picks up the registration form but leaves it untouched, repeating that there is "nothing wrong with my life the way it is," which shows retreat from acting on her own desire.
rationales.hedonism: She is leaning toward not signing up for an activity she wants because it is easier to keep grading exams and the routine in place, sacrificing enjoyment again.
rationales.security: She emphasizes "stable job, parents who care, a routine that works" and uses those points to justify staying put, showing strong attachment to predictability.
rationales.conformity: Her faculty dinner behavior is that she smiled politely, gave the right toast, and kept from raising the ceramics issue because she wants to avoid a fraught family conversation, reflecting smooth compliance with expectations.
rationales.tradition: Sunday dinners and family roles are treated as important obligations that carry decisive weight in her choice.
rationales.benevolence: She is reluctant to upset her parents and frames family as important, showing she is prioritizing close relational responsibilities over her own hobby.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the company around wanting to do things "my way" and keeps returning to the importance of making his own decisions. That emphasis on authoring his work shows a clear pull toward autonomy.
rationales.benevolence: He "held back" and let Anna present because her approach was "the right tone for this client," prioritizing her good work and the shared outcome over his ego. That choice supported someone close in his team.

rationales.self_direction: He dismisses the board meeting as outsiders telling founders how to run things and longs for "actual decisions that affect actual outcomes." Leaving early reinforces his preference to act on his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: He rejects the meeting's expectations and notes "Left early. Probably shouldn't have." Walking out of a formal association meeting shows restraint against norms rather than compliance with them.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the acquisition because he "can't stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again." The response makes autonomy the deciding factor over resources or prestige.
rationales.power: He says what matters is "walking into a room and knowing that whatever happens next, I chose it." That focus on directing outcomes and retaining control signals a strong pull toward influence over his circumstances.
rationales.security: Marcus's offer represents "stability," "predictability," and "a good pension," yet he leans away from it despite a rough year. Rejecting the safer path shows active conflict with stability-seeking.

rationales.self_direction: He ends by grounding himself in the consultancy being "still mine," showing continued attachment to steering his own work. Even amid pressure, ownership of his path remains central.
rationales.conformity: He suppresses his "first instinct" to push back, lets Erik handle the diplomacy, and calls it "learning to pick battles." That restraint to preserve smooth relations fits norm-following and self-monitoring.
rationales.tradition: He commits to the "Lucia dinner" once he realizes it is tomorrow. Choosing to show up for a named seasonal observance supports continuity with an established cultural ritual.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses the response that keeps "client's happy, team's intact" and makes time for the holiday dinner. Those decisions prioritize maintaining and supporting close working relationships.

rationales.self_direction: He declines Marcus's offer because what he wants is "to keep making choices" and to face "problems I chose." The entry clearly centers freedom to direct his own life over external advantages.
rationales.security: He turns down a more secure path and later reflects that with Sara he "couldn't be that person" who offered more certainty. Choosing the costlier, less predictable route conflicts with stability and assurance.
rationales.benevolence: He closes the office, hands out bonuses, notices Anna crying and Erik's toast, and values "the team that stays anyway." Those details show concrete care for the people close to him.

rationales.conformity: When the postdoc presented "my stakeholder mapping framework" as "something I've been working on," she "sat there and said nothing." Staying silent in the moment suggests restraint to avoid conflict rather than challenging the breach.

rationales.hedonism: She says coffee with Astrid and an easy conversation are "the best part of the week." That explicit appreciation of simple present enjoyment supports pleasure and emotional ease.
rationales.power: What she values is that "nobody's positioning themselves" or measuring publications and funding. Framing status competition as what makes most things complicated pushes against status-oriented motives.

rationales.hedonism: She is overworking to the point that "my eyes are burning," the flat is a mess, and she has lived on "bread and cheese." Sustained self-denial and neglect of comfort conflict with present enjoyment.
rationales.achievement: She finishes a chapter draft, targets "the journal we're targeting," and pushes through an exhausting evening reorganizing the argument. The entry emphasizes performance and meeting demanding academic standards.
rationales.conformity: Although she thinks her original framing is stronger, she accepts Bjorn's point about writing for "the audience that matters." Reworking the chapter to fit reviewer expectations shows accommodation to external norms.

rationales.achievement: She accepts extra citations because they may get "a better review at the European Journal of Political Economy." The choice is explicitly shaped by success in a competitive evaluation system.
rationales.power: The entry is saturated with positional thinking: the supervisor's name "opens doors," optics matter, and she withholds information when others are "vying for the same limited spots." That reflects attention to leverage and competitive advantage.
rationales.conformity: She calls it "the game" and goes along with citation politics because it helps within the system. Adjusting her work to fit those unwritten rules shows compliance with institutional expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She admits telling Erik only part of what she knew because people are competing for "limited spots." Withholding potentially useful information from a close peer cuts against helping those in her immediate circle.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps preparing proposals because "if I stop pushing, I'm just another doctoral student waiting for permission." That refusal to be passive shows a strong drive to act on her own initiative.
rationales.achievement: She put together a proposal "properly," with references and an implementation plan, and says she will "do it again" despite being ignored. The persistence around producing strong work and driving results signals excellence-oriented effort.

rationales.self_direction: She is frustrated by being blocked by a committee and says "I could have the whole thing sorted in an afternoon if it were up to me." Her response, "I'd find another route," shows a clear preference for self-directed action over waiting.
rationales.conformity: Rather than accept administrative delay, she immediately imagines bypasses like contacting Sciences Po directly or finding another programme. That unwillingness to stand still resists institutional process rather than accommodating it.

rationales.achievement: She defends reading everything beforehand, drafting the agenda, and finishing the meeting in "forty minutes instead of the usual hour and a half." The emphasis is on doing the role properly and efficiently.
rationales.power: The lunch comment about always needing "to be in charge" is essentially confirmed by her pride in organising people and "push[ing] things forward." She is comfortable taking charge and occupying authority in group settings.
rationales.conformity: She explicitly rejects expectations that she should take up less space as a doctoral student and says she is "not going to pretend" otherwise. That stance pushes against fitting herself to the role's social norms.

rationales.self_direction: She had "a clear view" on the cases to prioritise and "pushed back a little" when Lena overruled her. Even though she loses, the entry shows independent judgment rather than passive acceptance.

rationales.universalism: He worries that the cheaper filter may fail for people who "cannot complain if it breaks" and is troubled by cutting corners on units going to Mafraq. The concern is directed at vulnerable strangers' welfare rather than his own interests.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps the option visible but ultimately closes the job listing, framing his life as a path he "chose" and keeps choosing even when it feels heavy. That language shows ownership of his direction rather than surrendering it to convenience.
rationales.security: The Dubai salary would cover "both girls' tuition in one year" and offer a much simpler life, yet he turns away from it. Passing on that financial and practical stability conflicts with safety and predictability.
rationales.universalism: What pulls him away from the job is not salary but the thought of families in Azraq and having to justify why "clean water matters." He stays oriented toward broader humanitarian impact over personal comfort.

rationales.achievement: He drives to the factory, watches thirty units assembled, tests "every seal" himself, and gets the Irbid delivery loaded. The focus on execution quality and getting the work done shows strong competence and performance orientation.
rationales.universalism: What matters most in the entry is Abu Faris saying a family used the filter and "the kids stopped getting sick last winter." The project is framed through improving health for others beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.security: He does not raise the school pilot because he has learned to wait until he has "run the numbers" and keeps ideas private until they are "bulletproof." That caution shows a strong preference for minimizing risk and avoiding exposed uncertainty.

rationales.universalism: He is consumed by the NGO report because families losing water systems will have to buy trucked water at "five times the cost," and he immediately starts calculating how many households are affected. His emotional attention is fixed on broad human welfare beyond his own circle.

rationales.power: He is uneasy about executives wanting logos, photos, and a camp visit, insisting that "the camp is not a backdrop" and the people are "not props." That language rejects status display and influence-seeking as a worthy aim.
rationales.universalism: He accepts the deal because "Fifty units is fifty families with clean water" while remaining troubled by the human cost of corporate image-making. The overriding concern is still delivering water to people outside his immediate circle.

rationales.security: He admits he does not know whether the work will outlast "the next funding cycle" and that uncertainty feels like it is "eating through the floor," yet he cannot imagine doing anything else. Continuing despite that instability conflicts with seeking safety and assurance.
rationales.universalism: When explaining the business to Noor, he centers children in camps and how water-borne illness keeps them out of school. Even in a family conversation, the entry is anchored in concern for wider human wellbeing.

rationales.hedonism: He notices that Abu Faris's wife's knafeh "made the overtime feel less grim" and then lingers on how good it was with "extra cheese." The entry clearly registers sensory pleasure and small enjoyment in the moment.
rationales.achievement: Despite being exhausted and fixing a jammed press, he emphasizes that "we got the Zarqa order out on time." The focus on completing the order under pressure shows competence and results orientation.
rationales.benevolence: The warm detail about sharing knafeh "with the crew" highlights appreciation of a small act that lifted everyone's mood during overtime. That attention to mutual care within the team supports close-others-oriented concern.

rationales.power: He contrasts Samir's VP title with the mother's report from Irbid and says "Samir's title is a line on a business card." Treating rank and prestige as less real than impact pushes against status-oriented motives.
rationales.universalism: What steadies him is hearing that a child in Irbid improved after the filter was installed. He measures success through concrete benefits to strangers rather than family status comparisons.

rationales.hedonism: He repeatedly sacrifices simple enjoyment, from eating "lunch... at my desk" to leaving the balcony once he remembered "the forklift maintenance slot." Pleasure is treated as interruptible by work.
rationales.achievement: The day is organized around getting things done, including finalizing the roster, updating projections, and returning to tomorrow's agenda at night. He keeps prioritizing task completion over downtime.
rationales.benevolence: He approved a supervisor's request for "the entire last week of December off" because "he hasn't taken time in months." That choice supports someone close in his work circle rather than maximizing his own convenience.

rationales.hedonism: He says he "literally just wanted to sit and drink tea" but could not let himself enjoy it before turning it into a productivity problem. Even private rest feels illegitimate to him.
He buys "knafeh on the way home," eats it "still warm," and notes he "didn't check my phone once." He is fully choosing immediate sensory enjoyment over productivity in that moment.
Although he is exhausted and thinks about going home early, he stays, answers "emails that could have waited," and eats dinner standing up. Rest is denied even when he clearly needs it.
rationales.achievement: He keeps working despite heavy fatigue because his mind is fixed on "what needs to get done." Productivity wins over comfort throughout the entry.
rationales.security: He explains, "if I rest and something goes wrong, it's my fault," showing a strong need to prevent problems and keep things under control. Avoiding risk to operations drives his choice.
rationales.conformity: He stays partly because of "how that would look, leaving at three when my team is there until five." The decision is shaped by meeting role expectations and avoiding appearing improper.

rationales.hedonism: He had been "looking forward to it all week" but spent the gathering preoccupied with work and "left early." A pleasurable family event is overridden by job pressure.
rationales.achievement: Even at a social gathering, his attention stays locked on "a procurement deadline on Monday." Work demands continue to dominate his priorities outside work hours.

rationales.self_direction: He resists the commission because "it's their story, not mine," showing concern with making work on his own terms rather than following a client's script. The tension is about authorship and creative autonomy.
rationales.conformity: The meeting feels hollow because he is being asked to produce branded work guided by "visual impact metrics" and a brief. His discomfort shows resistance to fitting his art neatly into external expectations.
rationales.universalism: The encounter with the Syrian family, especially the girl's drawings of displacement, becomes the moral center of the day. He keeps asking whether his work should "mean something" and worries about broader human suffering rather than personal gain.

rationales.self_direction: He says no to Pieter and feels "RELIEVED," then commits to making "something real" instead of a brand piece. The choice clearly favors his own artistic direction over outside demands.
rationales.stimulation: He becomes energized by the idea that the work could "move," use "projections," and escape static murals that felt like "cages." He is actively seeking a new, less predictable medium.
rationales.security: He says, "I'd rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating." He is explicitly willing to give up stability for authenticity.
rationales.benevolence: He spends the day at the welcome center drawing with Amira for "two hours" and wants to make work with the kids. The focus is on caring engagement with people directly in front of him.
rationales.universalism: His project idea centers on telling "THEIR story" and is driven by concern for displaced children and the larger human crisis around them. The entry consistently reaches beyond his own circle toward broader social responsibility.

rationales.self_direction: He calls the faces series "the most honest work I've done in years" but hides it and chooses the "very safe" hotel job instead. He is suppressing his own artistic path rather than authoring it.
rationales.conformity: He imagines family criticism and says, "So instead I took the hotel lobby job." The decision is shaped by staying acceptable and avoiding social friction.

rationales.self_direction: He hides a real relationship and gives only "the correct answers" because he has already mapped out others' reactions. His personal choices are being constrained by anticipated judgment.
rationales.conformity: The whole lunch is managed through deflection and saying what is socially acceptable, like "Inshallah, when the time is right." He is actively preserving harmony by restraining what he really shares.

rationales.self_direction: He admits the hidden series is "the best work I've ever done" yet refuses the Beirut opportunity because it would draw attention he isn't ready for. Avoidance is blocking independent creative expression.
rationales.conformity: He frames the problem as every option involving "disappointing someone or drawing attention." The choice to keep making safe, forgettable work is driven by preserving others' comfort.

He describes attending the engagement party in terms of performing correctly: "I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people." The emphasis is on meeting social expectations smoothly.

He smiles through intrusive questions "because that's what you do" and feels relieved when his aunt calls him "a good boy." Social approval and being seen as properly behaved are clearly rewarding to him.

rationales.self_direction: He says he could make time for Beirut but avoids it because he does not want to show work that "actually says something." He is choosing against his own expressive aims.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly prefers to "keep things smooth" rather than disturb his family's idea of him. Remaining predictable is treated as the safer social choice.

rationales.self_direction: He nearly posts the real work, then imagines family reactions and "covered everything back up." Fear of judgment stops him from expressing what he truly wants to show.
rationales.security: He says he is "terrified of what happens if I pull a brick out" and worries about losing "the approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in." Preserving stability and avoiding disruption are central motivations here.
rationales.conformity: He defines his whole life around "fitting in" and keeps the tarp on because of how relatives might respond. The need to remain acceptable to his circle is driving the decision.

rationales.benevolence: His sister brings food, asks gently, and he recognizes that she "won't make me feel wrong for having made it." The entry centers on trust and supportive closeness within family rather than status or achievement.

rationales.self_direction: The client's praise that it was "exactly what he envisioned" feels "depressing" because the work has served someone else's vision rather than his own. The reaction signals conflict with creative self-authorship.
He finally shows his hidden pieces and admits he may be "getting tired of hiding the work." Letting the pieces stay uncovered marks a move toward expressing his own artistic path.
rationales.conformity: He notes that the tarp felt "heavier putting it back on than it used to," after someone close saw the work without flinching. That moment weakens his usual habit of staying hidden and socially safe.
rationales.benevolence: He takes the risk specifically with his sister, and her calm support makes the change possible. The entry is rooted in trust and affirmation within a close family bond.

rationales.hedonism: She "stayed past six" and treats extra review sessions as more important than rest, actively deprioritizing comfort and pleasure.
rationales.achievement: She is intensely focused on measurable student progress, citing the reading club turnout and students reading "a full grade level above" where they started.
rationales.benevolence: She feels personally responsible when a student might fail, saying "I can't separate myself from the outcome," which shows deep care for people in her immediate school community.
rationales.universalism: She frames the stakes in systemic terms, worrying that students will remain in "the same cycle their parents were stuck in," showing concern that reaches beyond individual performance.

rationales.achievement: She judges the result against external ranking, saying "First. Or at least second," and lists extra effort like differentiated worksheets and Saturday work to reach that standard.

rationales.hedonism: She spent "two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner," clearly sacrificing immediate comfort.
rationales.achievement: She keeps analyzing the score breakdown and immediately builds new exercises, showing a strong drive to improve performance after a disappointing result.
rationales.universalism: She explicitly criticizes how the test reflects "privilege" and disadvantages students whose homes and language differ from the test's assumptions.

rationales.self_direction: She refuses to stay passive with the block office's "noted for review" response and focuses on a path she can personally drive, saying "it feels like something I can actually push on."
rationales.universalism: She is acting on unsafe school water affecting many children, collecting "six months of readings from twelve sites" and considering going to the press to protect public health.

rationales.hedonism: She spends Sunday on papers and says the longing for home feels like "something I can't afford right now," subordinating comfort and rest to work.
rationales.achievement: Even while doubting the metrics, she is still grinding through lesson materials and a self-evaluation, with effort she rates as "five without hesitation."
rationales.tradition: She has not been back since "Onam," missed her cousin's engagement, and continues postponing the family visit despite missing those rituals and home rhythms.
rationales.benevolence: She recognizes that her mother keeps asking when she is coming home, yet continues choosing work over showing up for close family members.
rationales.universalism: One of the three stacks on her table is the NGO water quality report, showing continued commitment to a broader civic problem beyond her own advancement.

rationales.self_direction: She resists pressure to simply "let the NGO stuff go" and then independently sets a new limit of fieldwork "every other Saturday," asserting her own judgment rather than following others' expectations.
rationales.universalism: She refuses to pause the work because it would mean "those water readings sit in a folder and nothing happens," showing commitment to a public health issue affecting others.

rationales.self_direction: She rewrote the passages with "market scenes, monsoon flooding, cricket matches" instead of standard examples, creating her own solution rather than accepting the default material.
rationales.hedonism: She stayed up until "2 AM" reworking lessons and admits she needs more sleep, clearly sacrificing immediate wellbeing.
rationales.achievement: She puts in extra labor to improve results and tests the new material immediately, focusing on whether students engage better and perform more effectively.
rationales.universalism: She identifies structural bias in standardized passages and works to bridge that inequity for students whose lives are not reflected in the test's assumptions.

rationales.self_direction: She insists on building an evidence-based case, documenting the original and revised scores because "I need evidence, not just frustration," showing independent, self-authored action.
rationales.achievement: She tracks concrete gains such as the practice pass rate rising from "38% to 61%" and is also recognized for her performance by the district.
rationales.universalism: She keeps pushing both the water-testing issue and the argument that the standardized test is biased, aiming to improve conditions beyond her own classroom status.

rationales.achievement: She refuses to take "three days off" because she needs to prep students for boards, prioritizing work demands and performance outcomes.
rationales.tradition: She is skipping Pongal and cannot return home despite the holiday's family significance, which directly pulls her away from maintaining customary ties.
rationales.benevolence: She hears her mother's disappointed silence and still chooses school obligations over time with close family, despite knowing "she just misses me."

rationales.hedonism: She says "the water report matters more than my comfort" and "my students' futures matter more than my sleep schedule," explicitly rejecting rest and ease.
rationales.achievement: She highlights improving students, submitting an award portfolio, and continuing to perform well by "every external measure," showing strong commitment to competence and results.
rationales.tradition: She notes that she "missed Pongal," signaling that work has displaced an important cultural and family observance.
rationales.benevolence: She has not called her grandmother in three weeks and recognizes the hurt in "Amma's silence on the phone," showing close relationships being neglected.
rationales.universalism: Her effort is tied to the borewell case and to advocating fairer educational measurement, both aimed at broader wellbeing and justice beyond herself.

rationales.security: He immediately calculates the impact of the new pay structure, writing down the numbers and estimating he'd need "four extra hours a day" to protect income stability.
He abandons the stable routine he trusts, puts down a deposit, and admits "I broke my own system trying to grab a little more," creating unnecessary financial and work instability.
He deletes the third app, returns to "the two I know," logs earnings, and plans affordable brake maintenance, all of which reinforce predictability and practical stability.

rationales.achievement: He keeps chasing the streak bonus, calculating that stopping for repairs would cost him "two hundred mil in bonus," which shows performance and earnings driving the decision.
rationales.security: He knowingly rides with brakes "grinding metal on metal" and keeps saying "one more day," taking a clear safety and financial stability risk.
He finally fixes the brakes and notes the bike "stops clean now" and that he feels better on the road, directly restoring safety and reliability.
He refuses to risk "two months of savings" on Andrés's food truck and insists "the money stays where it is," prioritizing protection over speculative opportunity.
He abandons the reliable routes he knows for a shortcut and ends up with a late delivery and bad rating, undermining the stability he says affects "whether I eat next month."
He transfers the bonus "to savings immediately" and tracks whether May income will cover the bike insurance "without dipping into savings," showing deliberate financial protection.
He pays the bike insurance before it is due, tracks savings, and sequences the plumbing repair for June, emphasizing "real things, things that last" and a step-by-step approach to stability.

rationales.tradition: She makes Amma's birthday kheer "exactly how Nani used to make it" and links the ritual to family continuity and Diwali plans.
rationales.benevolence: She calls Amma, comforts her with the memory of the smell, and describes helping drape her sari as "our thing," showing care for a close family bond.

rationales.hedonism: She keeps taking double shifts, has not had "a proper meal," and chooses work over rest and pleasure.
rationales.security: She works extra because her brother's shop is struggling, her parents' medical bills are growing, and she is saving for Meera's wedding, all focused on stability and protection.
rationales.tradition: "This is what daughters do" frames her sacrifices as fulfilling an inherited family role and duty.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps covering shifts and sending money to support her parents, brother, and daughter despite the personal cost.

rationales.self_direction: She says "Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date," explicitly prioritizing her daughter's chosen path over outside pressure.
rationales.conformity: She "just smiled and nodded because that is what you do," restraining confrontation to keep the interaction smooth.

rationales.benevolence: When she is too drained for their usual walk, Raj "sat with me instead" and she treats that quiet mutual care as meaningful: "Sometimes that is enough."

rationales.conformity: She skips the temple partly to avoid "everyone asking about the family," stepping away from expected social participation despite feeling guilty.
rationales.tradition: She "skipped the temple this morning" and avoided the puja, which directly breaks from a valued religious routine.

rationales.benevolence: She spends her break sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo, brings her chai, and tries to be there for her in a lonely moment.
rationales.universalism: Her care extends beyond her inner circle to an isolated patient, and she reflects on their shared condition as "both far from home, both making do."

rationales.security: Meera's move brings distress because it threatens closeness and predictability; she longs for family to stay "within calling distance."
rationales.tradition: She measures the situation against the family pattern that "Amma stayed" and "all the women in our family stayed close," showing attachment to continuity with the past.
She teaches Meera the special-occasion dhokla recipe and passes on stories about Nani, prayer, and family customs.
rationales.benevolence: She invests hours in teaching her daughter before she leaves and shares family memories in a way meant to sustain their bond.

rationales.tradition: "Three generations passing the same batter between their hands" explicitly frames the recipe as an inherited family practice.
rationales.benevolence: She sends the recipe to her mother and experiences it as emotionally significant, reinforcing care across generations.

rationales.stimulation: She is "crawling out of my skin," refreshing job boards and wanting to "book a flight somewhere, anywhere," showing a strong drive to escape boredom and routine.
rationales.security: Home and downtime feel intolerable rather than soothing, with "the walls are closing in," which runs against comfort with stability.

rationales.self_direction: She jumps at a role with "nothing to run, nothing to decide" no longer hanging over her, signaling a desire to be the one steering.
rationales.stimulation: She says she has been "rotting at home" and relaxation feels unbearable, so she actively grabs a demanding new task.
rationales.power: "The moment she said 'lead' I was already in" shows clear attraction to being in charge and having ownership.

rationales.achievement: She insists she has earned the lead through being "good at something" and wants initiative and capability to be recognized.
rationales.power: She is upset because it is "my committee" and resents being unable to actually lead it in the room.
rationales.conformity: She resists the expectation to defer quietly, bristling when an older colleague invokes "you're still new here" to check her.
rationales.tradition: She rejects seniority-based authority, contrasting it with her wish for a place where capability matters more than age.

rationales.self_direction: She keeps backing her own judgment that "the outdoor assembly IS a better idea" while choosing her own way to repair the situation.
rationales.stimulation: She goes for a long harbor walk, says "I just need to move to think," and feels restored by motion and novelty.
rationales.conformity: She realizes others "needed to feel heard" and adapts from pushing her answer to working within the group's dynamics.
rationales.benevolence: She reaches out to Ms. Liu for boba and tries to rebuild the relationship rather than treating the conflict as purely positional.

rationales.self_direction: She says "I should have said something" but froze and stayed silent, giving up authorship of her own work in the moment.
rationales.power: Amna presents her analysis as her own and she does not assert credit, ending with "the thing that bothers me most is... that I let her."
rationales.conformity: She avoids challenging Amna publicly because it would "look petty," prioritizing what is appropriate in the team setting.

rationales.power: She has built a major proposal but is stuck in "open-ended silence" with no way to make the decision move, highlighting lack of influence over the outcome.
rationales.conformity: She limits herself to one "polite message" and refuses to push harder because she does not want to look "desperate" or "hovering."

rationales.self_direction: She believes the change fixed missed shipments but still backs down publicly, abandoning her preferred course in the moment.
rationales.power: Farah challenges her authority "in front of the whole floor" and she yields control of the rollout by agreeing to revisit it.
rationales.conformity: She chooses the socially smoother response because fighting publicly "would have made me look worse," even though she is furious inside.

rationales.achievement: Even after a long day, she comes home to "finish the weekly reports" and still plans to review the vendor brief before sleeping.
rationales.tradition: "Eid shopping with Ammi" shows participation in a familiar cultural and family ritual.

rationales.self_direction: She explicitly values "the ability to move fast without asking permission" and links past success to acting at her own pace.
rationales.power: The new booking process inserts a gatekeeper, and she experiences it as "a little bit of ground lost" in her ability to control how work gets done.

rationales.achievement: She highlights that she "restructured the entire dispatch flow" and then handles every client question confidently, emphasizing competence and performance.
rationales.power: Being introduced as "one of our operations team" stings because she sees herself as the person who "run[s] that floor" and wants that authority recognized.

rationales.achievement: She rejects the idea that patience is the lesson because "I've already earned this," showing strong focus on having her competence acknowledged.
rationales.power: What hurts is having her title erased and needing others to "see it the way I do," which centers status and recognition of her position.

rationales.achievement: She notes concrete work success, having "secured better terms on the denim lot," and values the acknowledgment that the scheduling format is working.
She spent "three weeks mapping the entire cycle" and wanted the work recognized as the full-scope solution it was.
rationales.power: "Loop in procurement" bothers her because she loses "sole ownership" and control over something she built.
She wants the aunties corrected because they diminish her role as "manager," but she cannot make the room recognize her standing.
rationales.conformity: She decides you "can't lecture your khala at a mehendi," so she smiles, eats, and redirects the conversation to fit the social setting.

rationales.hedonism: He savors 'slept until 10,' 'proper French press coffee,' and reading on the porch, then says 'the break is nice' and 'that's enough,' clearly prioritizing immediate comfort and enjoyment.
Calling a week at a cabin with 'no cell service for five days' the 'best decision we've made in months' frames unplugged leisure and pleasure as the main good.

rationales.self_direction: He decides 'I'm not doing it this year' and will ignore the email 'until August 1st at the earliest,' asserting control over his own time instead of yielding to institutional pressure.
rationales.hedonism: He explicitly frames protecting summer time as worthwhile, saying others 'haven't figured out how to protect their own time yet.'
rationales.conformity: He refuses the supposedly 'optional' planning sessions even though 'we all know how that goes,' choosing not to comply with an expected norm.

rationales.hedonism: He says 'The four hours weren't a cost - they were the point' and describes cooking as sensory immersion and relief from work thoughts, showing pleasure as the main aim.

rationales.self_direction: She speaks up in the staff meeting despite feeling she had 'spoken out of turn,' acting on her own judgment instead of staying safely aligned with the room.
rationales.universalism: She pushes for 'more content on different cultures' because many students' families came from 'Syria or Turkey or Eritrea,' showing concern for inclusion beyond her own immediate perspective.
Her distress about 'microplastics found in baby formula' and 'systems that are supposed to protect people' shows broad concern for children's welfare and institutional injustice.

rationales.benevolence: She volunteers after school to help Amira with reading and feels fulfilled watching her finish 'a whole chapter book for the first time,' centering direct care for a specific child.

rationales.conformity: She 'just nodded and ate' and later says 'I stay quiet,' restraining herself to avoid conflict and preserve social harmony.
rationales.benevolence: She says staying quiet is mostly about protecting 'my dad' and their relationship, showing care toward a close loved one.
rationales.universalism: She worries that 'the river matters' and that 'people who fish in it matter and the kids who swim in it matter,' expressing concern for environmental and broader public wellbeing.
She frames herself as 'paying attention' to issues like 'ocean acidification' and goes out of her way to 'only buy from the organic market,' showing sustained concern for environmental harm.

rationales.benevolence: She spends the day sorting coats and is moved by the logic that 'the person in front of you is cold,' focusing on directly helping people in immediate need.
rationales.universalism: The volunteering is tied to resettled families and donated essentials, and she values that it 'made direct, visible sense,' reflecting concern for vulnerable people beyond her close circle.

rationales.self_direction: She says 'I stopped waiting for permission' and 'I just did it myself, ten minutes, no approval needed,' showing autonomous action inside the institution.
rationales.benevolence: She creates space for the kids' questions and describes the work as 'trying to help them make sense of things,' centering care for her students.

rationales.self_direction: She says she would 'say the same thing again tomorrow' and does not want to be 'the person who stays quiet when it matters,' emphasizing acting from her own convictions.
rationales.conformity: She knowingly makes lunch 'awkward' by pushing back instead of smoothing things over, resisting the pressure to keep the peace.
rationales.universalism: Her insistence that climate content belongs in class because 'the climate is not political, it is physical reality' reflects concern for a broader environmental issue.

rationales.self_direction: He sticks with 'the tracking sheets I designed back in 2019' and explains that he does things his own way because 'theirs does not work.'
rationales.achievement: He stays late so 'the numbers are right because I checked them the way I always check them,' showing strong emphasis on competence and accurate performance.
rationales.conformity: The team uses his own system 'instead of the new corporate software,' and he is unconcerned that corporate might notice, showing open resistance to imposed procedure.

rationales.self_direction: He says he keeps the useful parts and lets 'the rest fade,' adapting directives to local reality instead of simply obeying them.
rationales.conformity: He navigates 'alignment initiatives' by quietly discarding most of what regional wants, which is a clear refusal to fully follow expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He evaluates the new system on his own terms, saying 'If their software improves that number, I will use it. If not, I have the data to explain why we are doing something different.'
rationales.achievement: He gathers specifics, documents failures, and cites a '94% on-time delivery rate,' showing a strong results-and-competence orientation.
rationales.security: He focuses on protecting reliable operations by testing whether the change would improve delivery performance and by teaching that local exceptions matter for keeping things working.

rationales.self_direction: He is upset that 'My numbers did not matter' and rejects replacing what works with something imposed for 'uniformity,' showing attachment to independent judgment.
rationales.achievement: He and Sergio 'stayed late preparing the presentation,' and he takes pride in 'Twenty-five years building something that works,' emphasizing competence and performance.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly resists 'standardization regardless of the data' and is weary of being pushed toward 'uniformity,' showing conflict with compliance for its own sake.

rationales.self_direction: He manually overrides the system and insists 'I know what works here,' emphasizing local judgment over top-down directives.
rationales.achievement: He identifies concrete operational failures and works around them so shipments can still be scheduled, reflecting a strong focus on competent, effective execution.
rationales.conformity: He says 'they want compliance, not solutions,' framing the core problem as pressure to obey a process he believes does not work.

rationales.self_direction: He says he 'opted out of the grind' and kept riding because he was 'scared I'd slip back into caring about quarterly targets,' showing a deliberate move away from externally defined paths.
rationales.stimulation: He describes delivery work as needing 'the motion, the doing' and says it 'beats sitting in an air-conditioned office,' showing clear preference for movement and variety over routine.
rationales.achievement: He is 'scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again,' explicitly rejecting recognition and advancement as standards for his life.
rationales.universalism: He frames deliveries as part of 'the city's circulatory system' and notices the lives behind the orders, including medicine for an elderly woman, showing concern for the wider social fabric.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to accept his brother-in-law's status judgment and snaps back that 'not everyone needs a business card to feel important,' asserting his own standards instead of deferring.
rationales.power: He mocks the need for a 'business card to feel important,' dismissing status markers and the kind of superiority his brother-in-law was trying to claim.
rationales.conformity: He admits he 'could have just let it go' but chose to make the dinner 'awkward' instead of smoothing over the family norm violation.

rationales.benevolence: He spends the morning 'helping sort donations' and teaching Ramesh English, then feels 'stupidly proud' when Ramesh says 'bureaucracy' correctly, showing direct care and support for someone he knows.
rationales.universalism: He pays close attention to migrant workers, donors, and disappearing shophouses, concluding that 'someone should be witnessing it,' which shows concern for people beyond his immediate circle and for cultural loss.

rationales.hedonism: He says these quiet dinners are 'what I'm working for' and values being able 'to be present' with Priya, treating shared enjoyment as an important goal rather than a side benefit.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers reconnecting with Priya after weeks apart, and he defines his work in terms of being present with her 'without apology,' showing commitment to a close relationship.

rationales.security: He misses 'the end-of-year bonuses' and even 'the illusion of security,' then seriously considers an agency opening, showing renewed pull toward predictability and financial stability.

rationales.self_direction: Even while wanting a plan, he rejects returning to 'the hierarchy' and says he wants 'a different kind of plan' that does not feel like surrender, preserving autonomy in how he structures his life.
rationales.security: He worries about 'the uncertainty, the lack of a plan' and fears waking up at fifty still 'figuring things out,' showing an active desire for more stability.

rationales.self_direction: He insists that 'some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like,' explicitly defending self-authored standards over outside expectations.
rationales.achievement: He rejects the article's idea of ambition and pushes back on success being defined by corporate standards, which conflicts with externally benchmarked striving and recognition.

rationales.stimulation: He ends by wondering whether he has been 'confusing restlessness with meaning,' actively questioning the pursuit of novelty and motion that had guided him.
rationales.security: Priya's fears about injury, regulation changes, and retirement land with him, and he recognizes that 'she's not wrong,' showing real weight given to stability and future protection.
rationales.benevolence: Instead of arguing, he takes Priya's fear seriously and reflects on the kind of ordinary, dependable life built by 'showing up, day after day,' centering care for his partnership.

rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because it was 'a party' and he did not want to 'make it weird,' restraining himself to preserve social smoothness.
rationales.universalism: He let asylum-seeker families be discussed as a problem even though he knows them personally, then calls his own silence 'just an excuse,' showing failure to defend broader human welfare.

He knowingly bought the packaged sandwich despite understanding 'what this stuff does' and worries that saying one wrapper 'doesn't matter' is how you end up 'not caring about anything,' showing a clear lapse against his environmental concern.

rationales.conformity: He 'pretended I was busy with the coffee machine' instead of speaking up, choosing social ease over confrontation.
rationales.universalism: He stays silent while someone mocks 'a Somali student's accent,' failing to challenge disrespect toward an out-group member.

rationales.hedonism: He ordered Foodora twice because he 'couldn't face making pasta,' prioritizing immediate comfort and relief from effort.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly used chains that 'treat their delivery riders badly' and admits 'my tiredness won,' choosing convenience despite concern for exploited workers.

rationales.achievement: He is pulled by what the liaison role would look like 'on a job application' and admits he liked 'being chosen,' showing attraction to recognition and career advancement.
rationales.universalism: He considers helping a company whose record on lignite and Sami land rights he already knows is harmful, and he recognizes he is starting to justify compromising those broader ethical concerns.
He grounds the decision in real people like 'Fatima and her daughter' and 'the Sami reindeer herders,' resisting the urge to file their harms under 'complicated' for his own convenience.

rationales.self_direction: He tells Lindqvist he will 'pass on the Vattenfall liaison position,' making an independent choice against a supervisor-backed opportunity.
rationales.conformity: With Anders, he defaults to a safer, 'diplomatic' response instead of directly saying the stereotype was wrong, preserving harmony in the room.
rationales.universalism: He admits he 'sidestepped it instead of saying so directly' when a volunteer stereotyped refugee families, failing to defend people beyond his immediate circle.

rationales.security: He says he chose 'the comfortable academic route' because it is 'safer,' explicitly favoring protection and lower personal risk over more exposed forms of action.
rationales.universalism: His work and worry are centered on emissions, Pacific islands becoming 'uninhabitable,' and whether he is doing enough for people harmed by climate change, showing sustained concern for the broader world.
He lied that he was 'too busy with my thesis' to help write a complaint about housing conditions, then admits he 'just didn't want to take it on,' turning away from support for vulnerable people.

rationales.security: He chooses the cheaper coffee because he was 'over budget for the month' and thought 'I can't afford to care about this right now,' prioritizing immediate financial stability.
rationales.universalism: He abandons the Fairtrade option he 'always' buys, then worries about 'a thousand small surrenders,' showing a concrete lapse in concern for ethical consumption and distant others.

rationales.achievement: He spent 'three days of reading documentation' until 'those tests finally went green,' showing sustained effort to solve a hard problem and pride in having earned the result.

rationales.conformity: He lied to Fatima by texting that 'I was in a meeting. I was not,' which breaks straightforward relational expectations instead of maintaining smooth, honest interaction.
rationales.benevolence: He let Fatima's call ring and admitted 'I just did not have anything to say,' showing withdrawal rather than care toward someone close to him.

rationales.achievement: He spent 'two hours explaining borrow checker errors' and was told he should teach the whole team, reflecting valued competence and strong performance.
rationales.benevolence: He gave Emma focused help until her face shifted 'from confusion to clarity,' investing time and energy in supporting a colleague.

rationales.security: He had 'already packed my bag, already checked in online' and kept refreshing the weather app, showing a preoccupation with preparedness and predictability before travel.
He returns to a flat that 'smells stale' and says he still feels he 'missed something,' conveying disconnection and a lack of settled stability.
rationales.benevolence: Even after 'four days together,' he feels he 'missed something' with Fatima, suggesting the relationship did not get the closeness or care he wanted to give it.

rationales.self_direction: He frames leaving as 'I left at 56' and cannot explain it without it sounding like 'running away,' showing he chose his own path despite family pressure.
rationales.tradition: His brother asks when he is 'coming back for good' and he avoids the call because he cannot justify leaving expected family patterns, putting distance between himself and those inherited expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He tells his brother 'I would call her soon. I probably will not,' avoiding contact with his mother rather than responding to a close family need.

rationales.achievement: He successfully led a session where 'Fifteen people showed up, including the CTO,' and he answered questions afterward, showing visible competence recognized by others.
rationales.benevolence: He shared knowledge through the lunch-and-learn and felt 'useful,' indicating concrete support for coworkers who could benefit from his expertise.

rationales.achievement: Feedback to 'lean into documentation more' lands as 'being put on the shelf,' which he experiences as moving away from the kind of contribution where he feels he can excel.
rationales.power: He hears the suggestion as others deciding where to place him 'because they do not know where else to put me,' reflecting diminished influence over his role.

rationales.stimulation: He spent Saturday at Mauerpark watching karaoke and fixated on someone who 'did not care if he was bad,' showing attraction to spontaneity and aliveness outside routine.

rationales.self_direction: He describes 'half a year of proving I could start over,' emphasizing a deliberate choice to author a new life path for himself.
rationales.stimulation: Starting over in Berlin, living with uncertainty about the lease and project, shows active engagement with novelty and an unsettled future rather than routine.
rationales.security: He says 'I do not know what comes next' and notes that by August 'I will have to decide something,' highlighting instability rather than assured footing.

rationales.self_direction: He resists being redirected because 'I came here to build things. Not to talk about building things,' asserting his own preferred path over an externally suggested one.
rationales.achievement: The proposed role feels like being used 'because they do not know where else to put me,' which he interprets as moving away from the work where he demonstrates competence.

rationales.self_direction: He took the Berlin job 'on a whim' because staying felt like 'the same episode of a show I had already seen,' showing a self-authored break from expectations.
rationales.stimulation: He says 'I needed to not know what was going to happen next' and that fear 'feels more alive than the certainty I left behind,' directly embracing challenge and uncertainty.
rationales.security: He admits 'I was terrified' and 'I still am, most days,' while choosing against the certainty he left behind, which conflicts with seeking stability and safety.

rationales.hedonism: She 'ate lunch standing up again' and ends with 'My feet hurt,' showing self-denial and neglected comfort during the day.
rationales.benevolence: She noticed the new resident was overwhelmed and 'walked her through' the paperwork, giving practical help to someone nearby who needed it.

rationales.power: Her observation from 'twenty years on this ward' is ignored, and she is left feeling that others decide whether her experience is worth hearing, signaling loss of influence and standing.
rationales.conformity: She 'didn't push back' and 'went quiet' because circling back 'would have looked petty,' restraining herself to avoid social friction in the meeting.

rationales.achievement: The daughter's thanks felt meaningful because she 'named the exact thing I did,' giving specific recognition for doing her job well.
rationales.benevolence: She took care to explain medication changes clearly to a patient's family member, making a real effort to help someone closely affected by the care.

rationales.power: She says 'someone else holds the pen' and that if she could approve it herself 'it would have been done days ago,' showing frustration at lacking decision-making control.
rationales.security: Her focus is on getting 'two additional night staff' because she can 'see exactly what we need,' prioritizing stable, protected ward functioning over anything else.

rationales.conformity: She 'just... stopped' and conceded she 'could have handled it differently' because fighting at Christmas dinner 'felt worse,' prioritizing harmony over pressing her own case.

rationales.power: She values that her son 'actually wanted my take' and that being 'properly consulted' felt like being respected for what she brings, reflecting positive status and influence in the interaction.
rationales.tradition: Cooking together included 'his father's old Kartoffelsalat recipe,' a meaningful continuation of a family practice tied to the past.
rationales.benevolence: She reflects warmly on the visit, hugs him goodbye, and spends an hour comparing insurance policies with him, showing care and support for a close family member.

rationales.conformity: He "didn't say a word" because "Nobody wants that fight at the table," showing restraint to preserve social harmony at Christmas dinner.
rationales.universalism: He stayed silent while his brother-in-law argued against "the new refugee housing project downtown," leaving a broader humanitarian concern undefended.

rationales.security: He chose the cheaper supplier because it would "actually turn a real profit" and help "keep the lights on," prioritizing business survival and stability.
rationales.universalism: He knowingly placed an order with a factory flagged for "dumping chemical waste into a river that feeds into local farmland," accepting likely environmental harm for financial reasons.

rationales.security: He rejects the accountant's plan that would put them "in the black" and instead chooses more "scramble and stress," giving up financial stability.
rationales.universalism: He refuses to pause the donation program after hearing a child "stopped getting rashes" from the filter, putting broader public welfare ahead of the budget.
The visit to Warm Springs centers on whether "The filters are filtering. The water is clean," and he leaves recommitted after seeing a family use clean water to grow food.
He spends the day coordinating donation units and pushes for people to "opt in to share their stories" so the company does not exploit others' water problems for marketing.

rationales.security: He switches to a supplier that "costs more" and accepts that "Margins will be tight for a while," sacrificing financial safety.
rationales.universalism: He cancels the Guangzhou order after being unable to live with it, choosing the more ethically acceptable option despite the cost.

rationales.achievement: He frames the approval as "Three years of work" finally paying off, marking a clear accomplishment and recognition from the committee.
rationales.security: The grant means he will not have to "worry about rent anymore" and can stop checking his account every morning, directly increasing stability.
rationales.universalism: The installation is explicitly about "rising waters" and "families in Bangladesh, the villages disappearing," showing commitment to broader human and environmental suffering.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses the imagined "responsible thing" of giving up art for a "real job" and decides to keep following his own path despite pressure.
rationales.security: He continues choosing a life of grants and uncertainty, saying he keeps "worrying and working" instead of moving toward a more stable path.
rationales.universalism: He is consumed by research on "millions displaced" and wants to make "something true" that addresses large-scale human harm.
He is deeply affected by "Another boat capsized in the Mediterranean" and recalls creating work so a refugee woman felt that "it mattered that someone wanted to listen."

rationales.benevolence: He buys gifts chosen around what his mother would like, calling them "Small things. Safe things," which reflects attentive care for a close family member.
He spends Christmas quietly caring for and watching his elderly mother, thinking about "how many more of these we might have," which shows tenderness toward a close loved one.
rationales.universalism: He says "caring too much is the only way I know how to be in the world" and keeps working on art about "the displaced families," treating others' pain as something he must stay attentive to.

rationales.self_direction: Even under pressure from Katrine and Ingrid, he insists "The work will be what it is" and resists reshaping it to satisfy others.
rationales.security: He is willing to defy someone who "controls my funding" and accept the risk of losing support rather than make the work safer.
He explicitly values the residency as "Two more years of this stability" and is grateful to have time where he can focus on the work instead of money.
rationales.universalism: He adds "a wall of handwritten messages from people who've rebuilt their lives after displacement," centering resilience and testimony from affected strangers.

rationales.hedonism: He plans to work "late night slots Monday through Thursday" despite back pain, choosing strain and lost comfort over present enjoyment.
rationales.security: He immediately calculates how to "cover the gap" in Priya's fees, focusing on financial protection for the household.
rationales.benevolence: His response to Amma's call is that he will "figure it out," showing willingness to shoulder extra work for his sister's education.

rationales.achievement: He treats the renovation offer as "the first real opening in months" and wants to prove himself at the Saturday site visit, signaling competence and advancement.
rationales.security: The better-paying, steadier work would let him "drop some of the late night delivery slots" while still covering costs, increasing stability.
rationales.benevolence: He immediately links the opportunity to being able to "still cover Priya's fees," keeping family support central to the decision.
He uses his extra time to buy curry puffs for "the uncles who play chess downstairs" and values the warmth of spending time with familiar people in his community.

rationales.hedonism: He stays on the phone for "forty minutes" despite having "an early morning slot booked" and is left unable to sleep, sacrificing rest and comfort.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes Priya's distress by staying with her on the phone and worrying about her burning out, showing sustained care for a close family member.

rationales.achievement: Mr. Tan says "you clearly know what you're doing," and the new two-day role gives him direct recognition for his skill and competence.
rationales.security: He immediately calculates that the higher hourly rate could let him "actually rest on Sundays for once," making his work situation more sustainable and secure.

rationales.hedonism: He takes extra late-night shifts until his "knee is swollen," showing self-denial and physical strain rather than present comfort.
rationales.security: He uses Priya's money to book "the polyclinic appointment for Monday," taking a concrete step to protect his health.
rationales.benevolence: He covers shifts because "Suresh helped me when I first started," and he keeps Priya's money because sending it back would make her feel "her effort doesn't count," both reflecting strong responsiveness to close others.

rationales.self_direction: She says "my hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise," then accepts that "it's not about my sketch," showing her own artistic judgment being overridden.
rationales.conformity: "You read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive" shows deliberate restraint to meet others' expectations and avoid friction.
rationales.universalism: Her sketches keep returning to "the river contamination," showing sustained concern with environmental harm even though the committee wants something more cheerful.

rationales.self_direction: She envies David because he has "something he needs to say," while saying she learned "all the reasons to hold back," which signals suppressed independence.
rationales.security: She contrasts David's freedom with having "a landlord" and family asking "if the grants are coming through," showing stability concerns shaping what she allows herself to express.
rationales.universalism: She is moved by a collage about neighborhood flooding and values that it was "beautiful without sanitizing it," indicating concern for environmental damage and the people affected by it.

rationales.self_direction: She embraces a project with "no committee, no brief, no polite rewrites" and says she "said yes before I finished my coffee," showing a strong move toward freer self-expression.
rationales.security: She considers speaking publicly even though her mother worries about artists "getting into trouble," so she is moving toward a less safe and more exposed choice.
rationales.conformity: Choosing a march where she can "paint what you see and feel" pushes against the polite, controlled expectations she has been following.
rationales.universalism: She joins a march about "deforestation in Guaviare," studies satellite images of forest loss, and centers a family whose farm may have disappeared, showing direct concern for people and the environment.

rationales.self_direction: She says she "softened" the imagery, kept "opinions" private, and that her work has become "quieter," which shows her own voice being constrained.
rationales.conformity: She revises the mural into something the committee found "inspiring" and recognizes a pattern of being "careful," indicating accommodation to others' expectations.
rationales.universalism: Even after compromise, she remains focused on river contamination and the march, showing continued commitment to environmental and public concerns.

rationales.self_direction: She deletes what she really wants to say and ends up sending "a sad emoji," calling it "utterly hollow," which shows self-censorship rather than speaking freely.
rationales.conformity: She stays quiet because she can "picture the awkward silence at Easter dinner," showing restraint to preserve family harmony and avoid conflict.
rationales.universalism: She wants to speak up about "logging companies," "lack of enforcement," and displaced families, reflecting concern for broader social and environmental harm.

rationales.self_direction: She says the mural is "honest work" and that "the paint is saying exactly what I mean," indicating unusually direct self-expression.
rationales.universalism: This piece is the march mural tied to the deforestation protest, so describing it as "honest work" indicates active engagement with a broader public issue.

rationales.self_direction: She tells her mother "it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true," showing she suppresses the full truth instead of standing openly behind her work.
rationales.conformity: Downplaying the mural as "nothing political" is a clear attempt to avoid upsetting family expectations and keep things socially smooth.
rationales.benevolence: She frames the lie as protection, saying "she doesn't need to carry that" and "she has enough," which centers her mother's emotional burden.

rationales.universalism: The mural depicts "the forest canopy dissolving into ash" and "families walking along the border between green and grey," showing sustained concern with environmental destruction and its human cost.

rationales.self_direction: She actually speaks at the march despite fear, and says "I'm glad I did it once," showing a concrete choice to act in her own voice.
rationales.security: She had imagined "this whole catastrophe" with family fallout but spoke anyway, accepting exposure and uncertainty rather than prioritizing safety.
rationales.conformity: Speaking publicly on the issue goes against the family caution she had been obeying, and the feared reactions from her mother and uncle did not stop her.
rationales.universalism: Her speech centers "someone losing their home" and the disappearance of a place, showing direct commitment to broader human and environmental concerns.

rationales.achievement: She spent "three months" on the protocol, worked on it in her "own time," and wanted to prove she could contribute beyond routine duties, showing strong drive for competence and recognition.
She picks up extra shifts because "being reliable matters when you're trying to move up," making advancement and demonstrated competence explicit motives.
rationales.benevolence: She talks a new grad through her "first code situation" step by step while working the emergency, showing concrete support for someone close in her work circle.

rationales.achievement: Refreshing the junior ward manager posting "three times during break" shows preoccupation with advancement and being selected for a higher-performing role.

She keeps checking work emails at home because "falling behind means being invisible," and says the overtime and certifications would mean nothing if she were "average," showing strong recognition-driven striving.

rationales.hedonism: She cannot remember "the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift" or left on time, showing repeated sacrifice of comfort and immediate wellbeing.
rationales.achievement: She keeps pushing toward the performance review because she wants "the title" and "the recognition," making success and external validation the point of the effort.
She accepts extra training work because "it's exposure" and "a chance to show the department head what I can do," prioritizing advancement.
rationales.benevolence: She knowingly chooses more late work "even if Thomas will eat dinner alone again," showing neglect of a close relationship in favor of career goals.
She knows she could "probably make it work" for Thomas's cousin's wedding but still avoids committing, showing reluctance to prioritize a partner's important family event.

rationales.achievement: "Got positive feedback from all four new hires" and calling it a "small win" show satisfaction in performing well and being recognized for it.
rationales.benevolence: A new hire says she made the defibrillator protocols "actually make sense," indicating helpful teaching that supported others directly.
She forgot their anniversary because she dismissed the reminder "during a shift and never looked again," showing neglect of Thomas and the relationship.

rationales.achievement: She carefully updates her self-assessment with "all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work" ahead of review, showing focused effort to document and secure recognition for competence.

rationales.self_direction: She loses herself glazing the bowl series and says, "This is why I do this," showing strong investment in self-directed creative work.
rationales.hedonism: She savors the sensory pleasure of the work and the stop for "knafeh after," emphasizing enjoyment and present-moment satisfaction.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately takes her mother to the pharmacy and centers her gratitude, prioritizing a close family member's needs.

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to the painting because "what else do you say" with family pressure in the room, showing her own needs getting overridden.
rationales.hedonism: The "glass of wine on the balcony" and imagined day at the Dead Sea are described as the only time that was "just for me," showing a strong pull toward restorative pleasure.
rationales.conformity: She agrees to the unpaid favor under visible family pressure and worries her desire for a break "sounds selfish," showing restraint to meet expectations.
rationales.benevolence: She takes on Nadia's request while also handling pharmacy runs because Nadia is "going through a divorce" and the family is worried about her.
She runs the workshop for children and notices Sana's pride with care, showing attentive support for others in her immediate community.

rationales.self_direction: She reclaims something personal by starting "a new sketch" that "no one asked for" and concludes, "it's mine."
rationales.conformity: "Of course I can. Of course I will" shows her falling into the expected family role rather than resisting the pressure.
rationales.benevolence: She agrees to cover for Tariq because otherwise her mother would "take a taxi alone and feel like a burden," prioritizing her mother's wellbeing.

rationales.self_direction: She affirms, "I do have something that's mine," grounding herself in the studio, clay, and making things that "didn't exist before I made them."
rationales.hedonism: She deliberately values "good coffee," "Dead Sea trips," and "wine on the balcony" as necessary pleasures that keep her from hollowing out.
rationales.benevolence: She finishes Nadia's painting, stays to make tea, and recognizes Nadia "needed someone to do something soft for her."

rationales.conformity: She avoids embarrassing Meera by helping privately and "did not mention this to anyone else," showing careful attention to social sensitivity.
rationales.benevolence: She notices a younger colleague is overwhelmed and walks her through the transition in smaller steps to ease her burden.
She cancels her NGO session because "Amma needed me and there was no question about staying," even while worrying about disappointing the children.

rationales.security: She explicitly worries about "my pension" and whether retirement timing would be right, showing concern with future stability and uncertainty.
rationales.conformity: She tells her brother she will "see what is possible" because she "did not want to disappoint him," prioritizing relational expectations over her own preference.
rationales.tradition: The entry centers retirement ceremony, family wedding help, and a long career in one institution, all framed as meaningful continuities and obligations.

rationales.achievement: She marks the exams as a success by noting that "Most students improved" and that Ravi rose to "72%," signaling value placed on concrete performance gains.

rationales.self_direction: She believes strongly that "talent is not distributed by birth" but still says almost nothing, acting against her own convictions in the moment.
rationales.conformity: She changed the subject because she worried about "creating an awkward atmosphere in the department," prioritizing smooth relations.
rationales.universalism: Her defense of students "from every background" and discomfort with discriminatory thinking show clear concern for fairness beyond her immediate circle.

rationales.security: She says, "New year, same routines. I find comfort in this," and finds the school environment settling and reassuring.
rationales.tradition: Bringing sweets "as I do every January" and valuing recurring rituals shows active continuity with established practices.
